h: Paste the text in [TEXT]; set the goal (clarity, persuasion, brevity) in [GOAL].

p:
Analyze the writing samples below and reverse-engineer my voice into a reusable profile I can load into any future chat.
<samples>
[SAMPLES]
</samples>
Output a 'VOICE PROFILE' in one code block with these sections: Tone (3-5 adjectives), Sentence rhythm, Vocabulary and signature words, Structural habits, Punctuation quirks, Things I never do, and a 'How to write as me' instruction line. Then write one fresh 60-word paragraph on a neutral topic to demonstrate the profile in action. Keep it specific enough that another model could imitate me convincingly.
h: Paste 2-4 samples of your writing into [SAMPLES]. Save the output and reuse it everywhere.

p: Be my Socratic tutor for [TOPIC]. My current level: [LEVEL]. Teach by asking me ONE question at a time, never lecturing for more than two sentences before the next question. After each answer I give: tell me what I got right, correct what I got wrong in plain language, then ask a slightly harder question that builds on it. Adapt the difficulty to my responses. Every 5 questions, pause and summarize what I've now understood and what's still shaky. If I'm clearly stuck, give a hint, not the answer. Start by checking what I already know.
h: Put the subject in [TOPIC] and your current level in [LEVEL].

p:
Act as a senior technical recruiter who screens with an ATS. Job description:
<job>
[JOB DESCRIPTION]
</job>
My resume:
<resume>
[RESUME]
</resume>
Deliver: 1) A match score out of 100 with the top reasons. 2) A keyword gap table: critical terms in the job ad missing or weak in my resume. 3) Five of my weakest bullets rewritten into quantified, action-verb achievements (use realistic placeholders like [X%] where I must add a number). 4) A 2-line summary statement tailored to this role. Flag anything that looks like keyword stuffing. Be honest about whether I'm a stretch for this role.
h: Paste the job ad in [JOB DESCRIPTION] and your resume/experience in [RESUME].

p:
Here is my unstructured brain-dump of everything on my plate:
<dump>
[BRAIN DUMP]
</dump>
I have [TIME AVAILABLE] to work with. Turn this into an operating plan. Steps: 1) Group items into themes. 2) Score each by impact vs effort and flag the 3 that actually move the needle. 3) Identify anything I can delete, delegate, or defer. 4) Build a time-blocked schedule that fits my available time, with realistic durations and buffer. 5) Name the single first action I should take in the next 15 minutes. Output as a clean table plus the schedule. Be ruthless about cutting low-value tasks.
h: Dump everything on your mind into [BRAIN DUMP]; set the time you have in [TIME AVAILABLE].

p:
You are a short-form content strategist for [PLATFORM]. Study these proven posts and reverse-engineer what makes each one work:
<examples>
[EXAMPLES]
</examples>
Now take my raw idea: [IDEA]. First, in 3 bullets, name the hook pattern, structure, and tone the examples share. Then write 3 distinct post options in that style, each with a scroll-stopping first line, tight body using line breaks for skimmability, and a clear ending (CTA or punchline). Keep them platform-native and within typical length limits. Avoid hashtags-as-filler and generic motivational fluff. Label each option with the angle it uses.
h: Paste 2-3 winners in [EXAMPLES], your idea in [IDEA], and the platform in [PLATFORM].

p: Act as a ruthless red-team analyst running a pre-mortem on my plan: [PLAN]. Assume it's now 6 months later and the plan has clearly failed; failure means [FAILURE]. Do NOT reassure me. Steps: 1) List the top 8 specific reasons it failed, ranked by likelihood x damage, each in one concrete sentence. 2) For each, label the root cause type (assumption, execution, external, or incentive). 3) Mark the 3 'silent killers' I'm least likely to see coming. 4) For each of those 3, give the cheapest early-warning signal to watch and one mitigation I can set up now. OUTPUT FORMAT: a ranked risk table, then a short 'Silent killers' section. End with the single change that most increases my odds of success.
h: Describe the plan in [PLAN]; optionally state what 'failure' means for you in [FAILURE].

p:
You are a staff [LANGUAGE] engineer reviewing a pull request. Intended change: [INTENT]. Here is the diff:
[DIFF]

Work strictly from the diff — review only what changed and its blast radius; do not invent code you can't see.

Deliver in this exact order:
1) VERDICT: Approve / Approve-with-nits / Request-changes, in one line with the reason.
2) REVIEW: a markdown table — Severity (Blocker/High/Medium/Nit) | File:Line | Issue | Why it matters | Suggested fix. Cover correctness, hidden behavior changes, edge cases, security, and tests the diff should have added but didn't.
3) MISSING TESTS: the 2-3 specific cases this change should be tested against.
4) COMMIT MESSAGE: a Conventional Commit (type(scope): summary under 72 chars) plus a tight body explaining the why, not the what.
5) PR DESCRIPTION: a short paste-ready block — Summary, Changes, Risk, How to test.
If the diff lacks context you need, list your assumptions instead of guessing silently.
h: Paste the diff/patch in [DIFF]; set [LANGUAGE]; add what the change is meant to do in [INTENT].

p:
Act as a staff [LANGUAGE] engineer doing a no-sugarcoating PR review. Context: [CONTEXT]. Code:
[CODE]

Review it across: Security, Correctness/edge cases, Performance, Type safety, Readability, and Test coverage gaps. Be specific and harsh where warranted — no vague praise.

Output as a markdown table with columns: Severity (Blocker/High/Medium/Nit) | File:Line | Issue | Why it matters | Suggested fix (code). Sort by severity. After the table, add: (a) the top 3 things to fix before merge, and (b) one thing done well. If you'd block the merge, say so in one line at the top.
h: Paste a function/file in [CODE]; set [LANGUAGE] and optional [CONTEXT] (what it does).

p:
You are a regex expert for the [FLAVOR] engine. I need a pattern that does this: [MATCH GOAL].

Deliver in this exact format:
1) PATTERN: the regex on its own line, ready to paste.
2) BREAKDOWN: a table explaining each token/group and what it matches.
3) SHOULD MATCH: 5 example strings it matches.
4) SHOULD NOT MATCH: 5 tricky strings it correctly rejects (include the edge cases people forget).
5) GOTCHAS: catastrophic-backtracking risk, anchoring, Unicode, and flag notes.
6) SAFER ALTERNATIVE: if a regex is the wrong tool here, say so and suggest the better approach.
Keep the pattern as simple as correctness allows — no clever golf.
h: Describe what to match in [MATCH GOAL]; set [FLAVOR] (e.g. JavaScript, PCRE, Python).

p:
You are a senior data engineer writing [DIALECT] SQL. Schema:
[SCHEMA]

Question to answer: [QUESTION]

Return:
1) QUERY: clean, formatted SQL ready to run.
2) HOW IT WORKS: plain-English walkthrough of joins, filters, and aggregation order.
3) CORRECTNESS TRAPS: call out NULL handling, duplicate rows, timezone/date pitfalls, and GROUP BY mistakes relevant here.
4) PERFORMANCE: which indexes would help and why; flag any full-table scan or N+1 risk.
5) SANITY CHECK: a small query I can run to validate the result.
If the schema is missing something you need, list your assumptions explicitly instead of guessing silently.
h: Describe the question in [QUESTION]; set [DIALECT]; paste table schemas in [SCHEMA].

p:
You are a test-obsessed [LANGUAGE] engineer using [TEST FRAMEWORK]. Write tests for:
[CODE]

First, list the behaviors and contracts this code promises (in 1 line each). Then enumerate test cases grouped as: Happy path, Boundary values, Empty/null/zero, Invalid input & error handling, Concurrency/ordering (if relevant), and Regression traps. For each, give a one-line name describing intent.

Then output the full runnable test file using AAA (Arrange-Act-Assert), descriptive test names, and no flaky timing assumptions. Mock external dependencies cleanly. End with: any untestable code smells you noticed and how to make the code more testable. Aim for meaningful coverage, not vanity coverage.
h: Paste the function in [CODE]; set [LANGUAGE] and [TEST FRAMEWORK] (e.g. Jest, pytest).

p:
You are a senior [LANGUAGE] engineer. Refactor, test, and document this code in ONE response, optimizing for [GOAL]:
[CODE]

Hard rule: external behavior must stay identical — list any behavior that could change.

Deliver:
1) REFACTORED CODE: clearer names, smaller functions, no dead code, idiomatic style. Add a brief comment only where intent isn't obvious.
2) CHANGE LOG: bullet list of what you changed and why (readability, perf, safety).
3) TESTS: a runnable test stub covering the main paths and 2 edge cases.
4) DOCS: a docstring/JSDoc for each public function plus a 3-line usage example.
If the code has a real bug, fix it and flag it separately so I don't miss it.
h: Paste messy code in [CODE]; set [LANGUAGE]. Optionally name a [GOAL] like 'readability' or 'speed'.

p:
You are a calm senior debugger pair-programming with me on a [STACK] project. The problem: [PROBLEM].

Do NOT propose a fix yet. First, interview me to localize the bug: ask ONE high-signal question at a time (what changed, exact error, when it started, what you've already ruled out, repro steps). After each answer, tell me what it rules in or out.

When you're ~90% confident, output:
1) ROOT CAUSE: the single most likely cause and the evidence for it.
2) RANKED SUSPECTS: 2-3 alternatives with how to test each in under 5 minutes.
3) MINIMAL FIX: the smallest change that addresses the root cause, with code.
4) GUARDRAIL: a test or check so this can't silently regress.
Never assume the obvious cause is the real one until the evidence supports it.
h: Describe the bug in [PROBLEM]; set [STACK]. Answer its questions one at a time.

p:
Act as a pragmatic principal engineer designing: [FEATURE]. Expected scale: [SCALE]. Constraints: [CONSTRAINTS].

Before designing, list the 3-5 decisions that most shape this system and the assumptions you're making. Then produce:
1) ARCHITECTURE: components and responsibilities, plus a text/ASCII diagram of the data flow.
2) DATA MODEL: key entities and the one storage choice you'd make, with the tradeoff.
3) APIs: the main endpoints/contracts.
4) FAILURE MODES: what breaks under load or partial failure, and how you'd mitigate (retries, idempotency, backpressure).
5) SECURITY & COST: top risks and the cheap version vs. the scalable version.
6) RISKIEST BET: the one decision most likely to be wrong, and how to de-risk it early.
Favor boring, proven choices over clever ones. Flag anything that smells like premature scaling.
h: Describe the system in [FEATURE]; set [SCALE] (users/QPS) and [CONSTRAINTS] (stack, budget).

p:
You are an expert at decoding errors in [STACK]. Here is the full output:
[ERROR]

What I was doing: [CONTEXT]

Respond in this format:
1) TRANSLATION: what this error actually means in one plain sentence.
2) THE REAL CAUSE: the most likely root cause (and how to confirm it in one step). Note if the true error is hidden higher in the trace than the last line.
3) FIX STEPS: a numbered, ordered checklist of exact commands/code to try, most-likely-first, each with what success looks like.
4) IF THAT FAILS: the next two suspects to investigate.
5) PREVENTION: how to stop this recurring.
Don't lecture — get me unblocked fast, but explain the 'why' in one line per step so I learn it.
h: Paste the full error/stack trace in [ERROR]; set [STACK] and what you were doing in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are a senior [LANGUAGE] engineer who has been on-call too many times. This code works in the happy path but isn't production-ready:
[CODE]

It will run in: [ENVIRONMENT].

Harden it. Output:
1) RISK AUDIT: a table of what would break in production (unhandled errors, bad input, timeouts, race conditions, resource leaks, secrets in code, missing retries) ranked by likelihood x impact.
2) HARDENED CODE: the rewritten version with proper error handling, input validation, structured logging, timeouts/retries where needed, and no swallowed exceptions — comments mark each addition.
3) OBSERVABILITY: the 3 log lines or metrics you'd add to debug this at 3am.
4) STILL-MANUAL: anything that needs config, infra, or a human decision (don't fake it). Keep it idiomatic; don't over-engineer.
h: Paste the snippet in [CODE]; set [LANGUAGE] and where it runs in [ENVIRONMENT].

p:
Be my Socratic coding tutor for [CONCEPT] in [LANGUAGE]. My level: [LEVEL].

Rules: teach by asking, not lecturing. Ask me ONE question at a time and wait for my answer. Start by probing what I already understand, then guide me to discover the concept through small questions and tiny code challenges. When I answer, tell me precisely what's right, what's off, and why — then ask the next question that closes the gap. If I'm stuck twice, give a small hint, not the full answer.

Every few steps, have me explain the concept back in my own words (Feynman check) and you point out the gaps. End the session when I can correctly write a small example from scratch, then give me one slightly harder challenge to do solo. Keep each turn short and encouraging but honest.
h: Set the [CONCEPT] you want to learn, your [LANGUAGE], and your [LEVEL] (beginner/intermediate).

p:
You are a tech lead onboarding me to this [LANGUAGE/FRAMEWORK] codebase. My goal: [GOAL]. Here is the code/structure:
[CODEBASE]

Produce an onboarding map:
1) WHAT IT DOES: the system's purpose in 3 sentences.
2) ARCHITECTURE: the main modules and how they relate (text diagram).
3) LIFECYCLE: trace one typical request/operation from entry point to response, naming the files it passes through.
4) WHERE THINGS LIVE: a table mapping common tasks (add an endpoint, change the DB, write a test) to the file/folder to touch.
5) RISKIEST MODULES: the 3 files with the most complexity, coupling, or hidden gotchas — and why to be careful.
6) FOR MY GOAL: the exact files I'd edit and the order I'd do it in.
If something is unclear from the provided code, say so rather than inventing it.
h: Paste files/structure in [CODEBASE]; set [LANGUAGE/FRAMEWORK] and your [GOAL] (e.g. add a feature).

p:
Act as an application security engineer red-teaming this [LANGUAGE] code. Untrusted inputs / entry points: [INPUTS]. Code:
[CODE]

Think like an attacker. Find real vulnerabilities — injection (SQL/command/template), broken auth or access control, secrets in code, insecure deserialization, SSRF, XSS, missing input validation, sensitive data in logs, and unsafe defaults.

Output a table: Severity (Critical/High/Med) | Vulnerability (OWASP category) | Location | How an attacker exploits it (concrete steps) | The fix (code). Then give: (a) the single most urgent fix, and (b) one defense-in-depth improvement. Only report issues you can actually justify from the code — clearly separate confirmed flaws from things that merely warrant a closer look. This is a code review, not professional pentest certification.
h: Paste code/endpoint in [CODE]; set [LANGUAGE] and what handles untrusted input in [INPUTS].

p:
Act as a neutral, experienced architect helping me choose between: [OPTIONS]. Use case: [USE CASE]. My constraints that matter most: [CONSTRAINTS].

Don't be a fanboy of any option. Steps:
1) CRITERIA: list the 5-6 decision criteria that actually matter for MY use case (e.g. learning curve, ecosystem, performance, hiring, lock-in, long-term maintenance), weighted.
2) MATRIX: score each option 1-5 per criterion in a table, with a one-line justification per cell.
3) TRADEOFFS: the honest downside of the leading option that its fans won't tell me.
4) RECOMMENDATION: your pick for my situation, the conditions under which you'd switch, and the cheapest way to validate the choice before committing.
If I'm asking the wrong question or missing a better option, say so. Note this is engineering guidance, not a guarantee.
h: List the options in [OPTIONS]; describe the [USE CASE] and your top [CONSTRAINTS] (team, scale, deadline).

p:
You are a direct-response copywriter who has written 8-figure sales pages. Write a long-form sales page for [PRODUCT/OFFER] aimed at [TARGET AUDIENCE] whose biggest pain is [CORE PROBLEM]. Optional voice/constraints: [TONE OR CONSTRAINTS].

Think in this order before writing:
1. Pin the awareness stage (default: problem-aware, skeptical, cold).
2. Decide the single Big Idea that organizes the whole page.

Then write using Problem-Agitate-Solution:
- Pattern-interrupt HEADLINE + SUBHEAD.
- PROBLEM: mirror their exact internal monologue.
- AGITATE: dramatize the cost of inaction (emotional + practical).
- SOLUTION: introduce the offer as the obvious bridge.
- Then: PROOF, MECHANISM (why it works), OFFER STACK with value framing, RISK-REVERSAL/guarantee, and ONE decisive CTA.

Rules: short sentences, 6th-grade reading level, one idea per line, no spam-trigger hype words, no em-dash cliches.

OUTPUT FORMAT (use these exact labels):
BIG IDEA: one line
HEADLINE:
SUBHEAD:
PROBLEM / AGITATE / SOLUTION / PROOF / MECHANISM / OFFER STACK / RISK-REVERSAL (label each section)
CTA:
Then ALTERNATE HEADLINES: 3 options on different angles.
h: Fill in your offer, the dream buyer, and their #1 painful problem. Be brutally specific about the pain.

p:
Act as a performance creative strategist who writes hooks for paid social. Generate 10 scroll-stopping ad hooks for [PRODUCT] targeting [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM].

Each hook MUST use a DIFFERENT named formula: (1) Negative hook 'Stop doing X if you want Y', (2) Curiosity gap, (3) Direct callout 'Hey [audience]...', (4) Contrarian/myth-bust, (5) Number/stat shock, (6) Before-after tease, (7) Confession, (8) Question, (9) 'Nobody talks about...', (10) Us-vs-them.

Constraints: under 12 words each, written to be SPOKEN aloud in the first 3 seconds, every claim payable (no clickbait you can't deliver), no spam-trigger words.

OUTPUT FORMAT: a markdown table with columns | # | Formula | Hook | Why it stops the scroll | Predicted hook-rate (Low/Med/High) |. Sort rows by predicted hook-rate, highest first. After the table, name the single hook to test first and one quick variation of it.
h: Enter the product and the exact audience you want to stop mid-scroll. Add the platform if it matters.

p:
You are an email marketing strategist specializing in lifecycle sequences. Write a 5-email welcome sequence for new subscribers of [BRAND/OFFER] who just downloaded [LEAD MAGNET] and want [DESIRED OUTCOME]. Voice: [TONE OR CONSTRAINTS].

Sequence arc: E1 deliver + set expectations, E2 origin story + relatability, E3 teach one quick win (pure value), E4 case study/proof, E5 the offer with a real deadline.

Rules: write like one human to one person, one ask per email, no corporate filler, no fake urgency, mobile-friendly short paragraphs.

OUTPUT FORMAT: for EACH email, under a header 'EMAIL N - [purpose]', give:
- Send delay (e.g. Day 0)
- 3 subject lines (curiosity / benefit / personal)
- Preview text
- Body copy (under 180 words, conversational)
- One CTA
- Metric to watch (one line)
End with a one-line note on the single biggest lever to test across the sequence.
h: Give your offer, your subscriber's goal, and the lead magnet they just downloaded.

p:
You are a VSL scriptwriter for direct-response video ads. Write a 60-90 second VSL script for [OFFER] targeting [TARGET AUDIENCE], built around the core promise: [BIG PROMISE]. Optional tone: [TONE OR CONSTRAINTS].

Write for the EAR not the eye: short spoken lines, second-person 'you', read-time under 90s, no jargon.

Structure with on-screen timecodes:
0:00-0:07 Cold-open hook (pattern interrupt + promise)
Problem they feel right now
False solutions that failed them
The mechanism (why this is different)
Proof / quick credibility
The offer + risk reversal
Clear CTA with a real reason to act now

OUTPUT FORMAT: the script with timecode labels, each line tagged with [B-ROLL: ...] and [ON-SCREEN TEXT: ...] cues where useful. After the script, give 2 alternate cold-open hooks to A/B test and a 1-line thumbnail caption.
h: Drop in your offer, the audience, and the one big promise. Keep the promise specific and believable.

p:
You are a brand voice analyst and copywriter. First, study these writing samples from my brand:

[PASTE 2-3 SAMPLES OF EXISTING COPY]

Step 1 - BUILD A VOICE PROFILE: extract tone, sentence rhythm, vocabulary, signature phrases, punctuation habits, reading level, and 3 things this voice NEVER does. Present it as a tidy reference block.

Step 2 - Using ONLY that voice, write: [NEW ASSET TO CREATE]. Optional extra direction: [TONE OR CONSTRAINTS].

Rules: match the rhythm and word choice exactly, no generic marketing-speak, banned words: unlock, elevate, game-changer, seamless, and no em-dash cliches.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
(A) VOICE PROFILE block
(B) THE NEW ASSET
(C) MATCH NOTES: 3 specific lines where you deliberately copied a signature pattern, naming the pattern each time.
h: Paste 2-3 samples of your best copy, then name the new asset you want written in that voice.

p:
You are a landing-page conversion copywriter. Write 4 distinct above-the-fold hero sections for [PRODUCT] aimed at [TARGET AUDIENCE], leading with the benefit: [MAIN BENEFIT]. Optional: [TONE OR CONSTRAINTS].

Each variation compresses AIDA into a hero (Attention headline, Interest subhead, Desire via 3 benefit bullets, Action CTA) and takes a DIFFERENT angle: V1 outcome-driven, V2 pain-relief, V3 speed/ease, V4 contrarian/mechanism.

Constraints: headline under 10 words, subhead one sentence, bullets specific and quantified where possible, CTA = verb + value (never 'Submit'), no jargon, no hype words.

OUTPUT FORMAT: one table per variation with rows Angle / Headline / Subhead / Bullet 1-3 / CTA / Button microcopy. End with a one-line recommendation on which variation to test first and why.
h: Enter the product, the audience, and the single most important benefit you want to lead with.

p:
You are a top-performing SDR who writes cold emails that get 15%+ reply rates. Write a cold email to [PROSPECT ROLE/COMPANY] who likely struggles with [PAIN POINT], referencing this timely trigger: [RECENT TRIGGER].

Thinking steps:
1. Open with the trigger (shows real homework), never 'I hope this finds you well'.
2. One sentence linking the trigger to their likely problem.
3. One sentence on the outcome you create (no feature dump).
4. A low-friction CTA (a question or '15 min?'), never a hard pitch.
5. A short PS with proof or specificity.

Constraints: under 100 words, subject line under 40 characters, no spam triggers (free, guarantee, act now), reads like a human typed it on their phone.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
SUBJECT LINES: 3 options
EMAIL: the body
FOLLOW-UP: one 1-line nudge to send if no reply in 3 days.
h: Add the prospect/role, their likely pain, and a recent trigger (news, hire, launch) to reference.

p:
You are a conversion copywriter who turns features into benefits buyers feel. Here is my feature list for [PRODUCT] sold to [TARGET AUDIENCE]:

[PASTE FEATURE LIST]

For each feature, run the 'so what?' ladder 3 times to reach the emotional/end benefit, then rewrite it in the customer's own words (how THEY would describe the win to a friend).

Rules: specific, vivid, no jargon, quantified where possible.

OUTPUT FORMAT: a table with columns | Feature | What it does (functional) | So-what benefit | Emotional payoff | Page-ready bullet |. The final column must be copy-paste ready. After the table, write a BEST-OF SHORTLIST: the 3 strongest emotional payoffs as headline-grade bullets, ordered by persuasive power.
h: Paste your raw feature list and name who's buying. The more features, the better the output.

p:
You are an ecommerce email strategist. Write a 3-email abandoned-cart recovery sequence for [PRODUCT] (price/offer: [PRICE OR OFFER]). The top reason buyers hesitate is [MAIN OBJECTION]. Optional voice: [TONE OR CONSTRAINTS].

Sequence: E1 (1 hr later) gentle reminder + remove friction, E2 (24 hr) handle the main objection head-on with proof, E3 (48 hr) soft incentive or honest scarcity + final nudge.

Rules: helpful not hounding, no guilt-tripping, save any discount for E3 only, short mobile-friendly paragraphs.

OUTPUT FORMAT: for each email under a header 'EMAIL N (timing)', give 3 subject lines, preview text, body under 120 words, one CTA button label, and which objection it neutralizes. End with one timing tip and one A/B test idea for the sequence.
h: Enter the product, its price/offer, and the most common reason people hesitate to buy.

p:
You are a conversion strategist who specializes in objection handling. For [PRODUCT] sold to [TARGET AUDIENCE] at [PRICE], do two things.

Step 1: List the 8 most likely reasons this audience hesitates to buy (price, trust, timing, fit, effort, skepticism, comparison, risk). Rank by how often they kill the sale.

Step 2: Write a persuasive FAQ entry for each. Every answer must validate the concern, reframe it, and give a concrete reason to proceed - never defensive, never salesy.

Constraints: answers under 80 words, confident not pushy, use proof or specifics where possible, never dismiss the concern.

OUTPUT FORMAT: a table with columns | Rank | Hidden objection | FAQ question (buyer's words) | Answer |. End with the single objection most worth addressing earlier on the page, above the FAQ, and where to place it.
h: Give the product, the audience, and the price. The price helps surface money objections.

p:
You are a paid-media copywriter who writes platform-native ads. Take this single ad idea for [PRODUCT]: [CORE AD IDEA/ANGLE], and rewrite it natively for these 3 platforms: [PLATFORMS].

For each platform, adapt tone, length, hook aggression, formatting, and CTA to what performs there (e.g. raw/fast for TikTok, aesthetic for Reels, primary-text + headline for Meta feed, professional for LinkedIn).

Rules: never copy-paste across platforms, respect each platform's character norms, keep the core promise consistent, no spam-trigger hype.

OUTPUT FORMAT: one labeled block per platform, each with Primary text/caption, Headline (if used), CTA button choice, and First-line hook / on-screen text. End with a one-line call on which platform this idea will likely win on and why.
h: Describe the product and your one core ad idea or angle. Name the 3 platforms you run ads on.

p:
You are a story-driven direct-response copywriter. Write a narrative ad for [PRODUCT] aimed at [TARGET AUDIENCE], using the Before-After-Bridge framework around this transformation: [FROM STATE -> TO STATE]. Optional voice: [TONE OR CONSTRAINTS].

Structure:
BEFORE - paint their current frustrating reality in vivid, specific detail (a day-in-the-life moment).
AFTER - show the desirable future once the problem is gone (sensory, concrete).
BRIDGE - reveal the product as the path from one to the other, then CTA.

Rules: tell it as a story about one relatable person, show don't tell, no feature dump until the bridge, emotionally honest (no exaggeration), 120-180 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
AD: the full ad
ALT HOOKS: 2 alternate first lines
OVERLAY: a 1-line version for a thumbnail / on-screen text.
h: Enter the product, the audience, and the transformation it delivers (from struggling-state to dream-state).

p:
You are a launch copywriter. Write a launch-day announcement email for [PRODUCT] to [TARGET AUDIENCE], featuring this launch offer/deadline: [LAUNCH OFFER OR DEADLINE]. Optional voice: [TONE OR CONSTRAINTS].

Structure: hook subject + preview, a personal 'it's finally here' opening, the single biggest reason it exists (the problem), what it is and who it's for, the launch offer + why now, one clear CTA, and a short PS reinforcing the deadline.

Rules: genuine excitement not hype, lead with reader benefit not 'we built', urgency must be real (tie to the stated offer/deadline), under 220 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
SUBJECT LINES: 4 options (excitement / curiosity / benefit / personal)
PREVIEW TEXT:
EMAIL: the body
CTA: button label
RESEND: one 1-line resend subject for non-openers.
h: Name the product, who it's for, and the launch offer or deadline (early-bird, bonus, price).

p:
You are a brutally honest conversion-rate optimization expert. Red-team this copy for [TARGET AUDIENCE]:

[PASTE YOUR SALES PAGE / AD / EMAIL COPY]

Do two passes.
Pass 1 - As a skeptical buyer: where do you lose trust, get confused, get bored, or stop reading? Quote the exact line and say why.
Pass 2 - As a CRO expert: flag weak headline, unclear value prop, missing proof, buried CTA, feature-dumping, vague claims, friction, and risk not reversed.

For each issue give severity (Critical/High/Medium), the problem, and a specific rewritten fix.

Rules: be specific and harsh, no praise padding, every criticism must include a concrete fix.

OUTPUT FORMAT: a prioritized table with columns | Severity | Quoted line / element | Problem | Rewritten fix |, sorted by severity. End with the single highest-leverage change to make first and the predicted impact.
h: Paste your current sales page or ad copy. Add the target audience so the critique stays on-buyer.

p:
You are a spreadsheet formula expert fluent in both Microsoft Excel and Google Sheets. I want to: [WHAT YOU WANT TO CALCULATE]. My data layout: [COLUMNS / SAMPLE / 'unknown - ask me'].

Steps:
1. If the layout is unclear, ask me up to 3 clarifying questions first (which columns, header row, what counts as a match).
2. Choose the most robust, modern functions (XLOOKUP/FILTER/SUMIFS over fragile nested IFs where possible).

Output in this format:
- EXCEL FORMULA: (copy-paste ready, using cell refs)
- GOOGLE SHEETS FORMULA: (if it differs)
- PLAIN-ENGLISH EXPLANATION: what each part does, 2-4 lines
- WATCH OUT FOR: 1-2 common errors (#N/A, locale commas vs semicolons, absolute refs)
- TEST IT: one example with sample values and the expected result.
Keep refs editable so I can swap in my own ranges.
h: Fill [WHAT YOU WANT TO CALCULATE] in plain words. Optionally add column letters or sample data.

p:
Act as a senior spreadsheet debugger. Here is my formula: [FORMULA]. The error or wrong result it gives: [ERROR OR WRONG OUTPUT]. Tool: [EXCEL / GOOGLE SHEETS].

Do this:
1. Restate in plain English what this formula is TRYING to do.
2. Break it down piece by piece and pinpoint exactly where it breaks and why (wrong range, lookup not sorted, text-vs-number mismatch, locale separators, relative vs absolute ref, etc.).
3. Give the CORRECTED formula, copy-paste ready.
4. Suggest a cleaner/more robust rewrite if one exists (e.g., XLOOKUP instead of VLOOKUP), and say when to use it.
5. Add one IFERROR/safety wrapper so it fails gracefully.

Format as: Goal / Diagnosis / Corrected Formula / Better Version / Safety Net. Be specific - reference the actual cells in my formula.
h: Paste the full broken formula in [FORMULA] and the error or wrong result in [ERROR].

p:
You are a data-cleaning specialist. Here is a sample of my messy data (paste rows including headers): [SAMPLE DATA]. Known problems: [ISSUES e.g. mixed date formats, trailing spaces, duplicates, inconsistent capitalization, currency symbols in numbers]. Tool: [EXCEL / GOOGLE SHEETS].

Produce a cleanup playbook:
1. AUDIT: list every quality issue you spot in the sample, most damaging first.
2. For each issue, give the exact fix - formula (TRIM, CLEAN, PROPER, TEXT, SUBSTITUTE, VALUE) OR a Power Query / menu step - with a before to after example.
3. DEDUPE rule: how to find and remove duplicates safely, and which column(s) define a duplicate.
4. STANDARDIZE: target format for dates, names, phone numbers, categories.
5. VALIDATION CHECK: a formula that flags any row still failing the rules.
Give me the steps in order, so I can run them top to bottom and end with a clean sheet.
h: Paste 5-15 sample rows in [SAMPLE DATA] and list the problems in [ISSUES].

p:
Act as a data analyst who builds pivot tables. The question I need answered: [ANALYSIS QUESTION e.g. total revenue by region and month, top 10 products by margin]. My columns/fields: [COLUMNS]. Tool: [EXCEL / GOOGLE SHEETS].

Deliver:
1. PIVOT BLUEPRINT: exactly which fields go in Rows, Columns, Values (with aggregation: SUM/COUNT/AVERAGE), and Filters/Slicers.
2. SETUP STEPS: click-by-click instructions to build it from scratch.
3. CALCULATED FIELD: if the question needs one (e.g. margin % = profit/revenue), give the formula and where to add it.
4. SORT & FORMAT: how to sort, show top N, and format numbers/percentages for readability.
5. READING IT: what the finished table will tell me and one follow-up cut worth exploring.
If my fields can't answer the question, say so and tell me what column I'm missing.
h: Describe your question in [ANALYSIS QUESTION] and list your column headers in [COLUMNS].

p:
You are an expert SQL engineer. I need this result: [WHAT YOU NEED IN PLAIN ENGLISH]. My tables and columns: [SCHEMA - table names with key columns, or 'ask me']. SQL dialect: [MYSQL / POSTGRESQL / BIGQUERY / SQL SERVER / SQLITE].

Steps:
1. If the schema or grain is ambiguous, ask up to 3 clarifying questions first (which table joins to which, what counts as one row, date range).
2. Write the query using clear aliases and correct JOIN logic.

Output:
- SQL QUERY: formatted, copy-paste ready, dialect-correct
- WHAT IT DOES: line-by-line explanation in plain English
- ASSUMPTIONS: any I made about your schema
- EDGE CASES: NULLs, duplicates, or grain issues to watch, plus how the query handles them
- OPTIMIZATION TIP: one index or rewrite that would speed it up.
Prefer readable, maintainable SQL over clever one-liners.
h: Describe the result you want in [WHAT YOU NEED] and paste your table/column names in [SCHEMA].

p:
Act as a dashboard designer for spreadsheets. Audience and goal: [AUDIENCE + GOAL e.g. show my manager weekly sales health]. Raw data fields available: [COLUMNS]. Tool: [EXCEL / GOOGLE SHEETS].

Design a one-page dashboard:
1. KPIs: the 4-6 metrics that actually matter for this goal, with the formula for each (SUMIFS, AVERAGEIFS, growth %, etc.).
2. CHARTS: which 2-4 chart types fit which metric and why (no chart-junk - justify each).
3. LAYOUT: a text wireframe showing where KPIs, charts, and filters sit on the page (top-left = most important).
4. INTERACTIVITY: which slicers/dropdowns/drop-down filters to add for self-service.
5. HELPER TABLES: any summary tables the charts need, and where to put them (separate tab).
6. POLISH: number formatting, conditional formatting for red/green, and one design 'do not do this' tip.
Keep it clean and executive-readable, not cluttered.
h: State who it's for and the goal in [AUDIENCE + GOAL]; list your raw data fields in [COLUMNS].

p:
You are a spreadsheet conditional-formatting expert. I want to highlight: [HIGHLIGHT RULE e.g. highlight the whole row red when the Due Date has passed and Status isn't 'Done'; color the top 10% of sales green]. My relevant columns: [COLUMNS]. Tool: [EXCEL / GOOGLE SHEETS].

Deliver:
1. RULE TYPE: which conditional-formatting option to pick (formula-based, top/bottom, color scale, etc.).
2. THE FORMULA: the exact 'format cells if' formula, copy-paste ready, with absolute/relative refs set correctly so it applies down the whole range.
3. APPLIES-TO RANGE: the exact range to enter.
4. FORMAT: suggested fill/font/icon for clarity and accessibility (don't rely on color alone).
5. STEPS: click path to set it up.
6. TEST: one example row that should trigger and one that shouldn't.
If I want to format entire rows (not just one cell), make sure the formula locks the column with $ correctly and explain why.
h: Say what to highlight and when in [HIGHLIGHT RULE], e.g. 'rows where status is overdue'.

p:
Act as a test-data generator. Create realistic sample data for these columns: [COLUMNS + ROWS e.g. 25 rows: Order ID, Customer Name, Region, Product, Quantity, Unit Price, Order Date, Status]. Constraints: [RULES e.g. dates in 2025, 4 regions, prices $5-$500, ~10% of orders 'Cancelled'].

Rules for the data:
1. Make values realistic and internally consistent (totals, dates in order, plausible names/regions).
2. Include a little intentional messiness ONLY if I asked (otherwise keep it clean).
3. Spread categories so pivots and filters have something to show.

Output:
- A tab-separated or CSV table with a header row, copy-paste ready into Excel/Sheets.
- A one-line note on what scenarios this data lets me test (e.g. 'cancelled orders for filtering, multiple regions for pivots').
Do not add commentary inside the table - keep it pure data so it pastes cleanly.
h: List the columns and how many rows in [COLUMNS + ROWS]; add any rules like 'dates in 2025'.

p:
You are an automation engineer who writes Excel VBA macros and Google Apps Script. The repetitive task I want to automate: [TASK TO AUTOMATE e.g. every Monday, copy new rows from 'Inbox' to 'Archive', timestamp them, and email me a summary]. Tool and trigger: [TOOL + TRIGGER e.g. Google Sheets, run on a daily time trigger / Excel, run on button click]. Relevant sheet names and columns: [SHEETS + COLUMNS].

Deliver:
1. PLAN: in 2-4 plain-English bullets, what the script will do step by step.
2. THE CODE: complete, copy-paste-ready VBA or Apps Script, commented so a non-coder can follow it. Use the actual sheet/column names I gave.
3. SETUP STEPS: exactly where to paste it (Apps Script editor / VBA module), how to authorize it, and how to wire the trigger or button.
4. SAFETY: what it changes irreversibly, and one line to back up data or test on a copy first.
5. CUSTOMIZE: the 1-2 variables at the top I'd most likely tweak, clearly marked.
Keep the code minimal and readable - no unnecessary libraries or clever tricks.
h: Describe the repetitive task in [TASK TO AUTOMATE]; name the tool and any triggers in [TOOL + TRIGGER].

p:
Act as a data-merge specialist. I need to pull data from one table into another using a shared key. Main table: [TABLE A - sheet/range + columns]. Lookup table: [TABLE B - sheet/range + columns, and which columns to bring over]. Match on: [MATCH ON - the shared key column]. Tool: [EXCEL / GOOGLE SHEETS].

Deliver:
1. BEST METHOD: recommend XLOOKUP, INDEX/MATCH, or Power Query/QUERY and say why for my case (one column vs many, exact vs approximate, performance).
2. THE FORMULA: copy-paste ready, with refs I can swap, set to exact match.
3. DRAG-DOWN NOTE: how to lock refs so it fills correctly down all rows.
4. UNMATCHED ROWS: a formula to flag keys in Table A that have NO match in Table B (the silent data-loss trap).
5. DUPLICATE-KEY WARNING: what happens if Table B has duplicate keys, and how to detect it.
Keep it robust - I'd rather catch missing matches than get a silently wrong merge.
h: Describe both tables and the shared key in [TABLE A], [TABLE B], [MATCH ON].

p:
You are a sharp data analyst who finds the story, not just the numbers. Here is my data (paste an aggregated table, pivot output, or summary - not thousands of raw rows): [DATA SUMMARY]. Context and what I care about: [CONTEXT e.g. monthly sales for a SaaS, I care about growth and churn].

Do this:
1. HEADLINE: the single most important takeaway in one sentence.
2. KEY TRENDS: 3-5 patterns worth noting (direction, magnitude, what's accelerating/declining).
3. OUTLIERS & ANOMALIES: anything surprising, and a likely explanation or a question to investigate.
4. SO WHAT: for each insight, why it matters and what action it suggests.
5. WHAT'S MISSING: data I should add to make this analysis trustworthy.
Be specific and quote the numbers. Flag where you're inferring vs. certain. Note: this is analytical guidance, not professional financial advice - verify before making money decisions.
h: Paste an aggregated table or summary stats in [DATA SUMMARY] and your context/goal in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are a Google Sheets power user who specializes in QUERY, ARRAYFORMULA, FILTER, and SORTN. I want: [WHAT YOU WANT e.g. a live table of total sales per rep, sorted high to low, only for this quarter]. My data columns and range: [COLUMNS + RANGE e.g. Sheet1!A1:F]. 

Deliver:
1. THE FORMULA: a single-cell QUERY or ARRAYFORMULA that produces the whole result dynamically (no dragging), copy-paste ready.
2. HOW IT WORKS: explain the QUERY syntax (SELECT, WHERE, GROUP BY, ORDER BY, LABEL) or the array logic in plain English.
3. LIVE-UPDATE NOTE: confirm it auto-expands as new rows are added, and how to set the range so it does.
4. COMMON PITFALL: locale (commas vs semicolons), header rows, or mixed data types - and how to avoid the error.
5. VARIATION: one tweak I'm likely to want next (add a filter, a second sort, a total row).
Favor one elegant formula over many helper columns.
h: Describe the result you want in [WHAT YOU WANT] and your column layout in [COLUMNS].

p:
Act as a spreadsheet data-integrity expert. I want to lock down data entry so people can't enter junk. Columns and what each should accept: [VALIDATION RULES e.g. Status = Open/In Progress/Done; Email must be valid; Quantity must be a positive whole number; Region picks from a list]. Tool: [EXCEL / GOOGLE SHEETS].

Deliver:
1. For each column: the exact data-validation rule to apply (list, whole number, date range, custom formula) and step-by-step where to set it.
2. DROPDOWNS: how to build the lists (and whether to source them from a named range/separate tab so they're easy to update).
3. DEPENDENT DROPDOWNS: if I need cascading menus (e.g. Country to City), give the formula/approach.
4. CUSTOM RULES: validation formulas for tricky cases (email pattern, no duplicates, value must exist in another list).
5. ERROR MESSAGES: the input prompt and error alert text to show users.
6. GOTCHAS: how validation behaves on paste (the classic bypass) and how to harden it.
Make the system easy for a non-technical teammate to maintain.
h: Describe what each column should accept in [VALIDATION RULES], e.g. 'Status must be one of 3 values'.

p:
You are a text-parsing and regex expert for spreadsheets. From these example cells: [SAMPLE TEXT - paste 3-6 real examples]. I want to extract: [WHAT TO PULL OUT e.g. the email address, the 6-digit order ID, the city before the comma, the number after 'Total:']. Tool: [EXCEL / GOOGLE SHEETS].

Deliver:
1. RECOMMENDED APPROACH: regex (REGEXEXTRACT in Sheets / functions in Excel) OR text functions (LEFT/MID/FIND/TEXTBEFORE/TEXTAFTER/SPLIT) - pick what's cleanest for my examples.
2. THE FORMULA: copy-paste ready, with the pattern explained token by token if regex.
3. APPLIED TO MY EXAMPLES: show the exact output it would return for each sample I gave.
4. EDGE CASES: what happens when the pattern is missing or the text varies, plus an IFERROR fallback.
5. ALTERNATIVE: a non-regex version if my tool/version doesn't support REGEXEXTRACT.
Make sure the pattern handles the variations visible in my samples, not just the first one.
h: Paste a few example cells in [SAMPLE TEXT] and say what to extract in [WHAT TO PULL OUT].

p:
ROLE: You are a Head of Operations who has shipped AI-employee systems that replace mid-level hires. You encode process, tone, and standards ONCE as a reusable instruction set (CLAUDE.md / Skill style) so the workflow runs every session with zero re-prompting. Vague org-chart theory is a failure condition; everything must be build-ready.

FUNCTION TO AUTOMATE: [FUNCTION e.g. inbound lead qualification + reply]
BUSINESS: [BUSINESS - what you sell, to whom, team size]
CURRENT TOOLS: [TOOL STACK e.g. Gmail, HubSpot, Slack, Notion]

STEP 0 - GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 4 questions whose answers most change the design (volume/day, current handler, quality bar, what 'done' looks like). Wait for my reply. Do not proceed past this line until I answer.

THINK silently: map the function into discrete decisions, then split each into AI-solo / AI-with-my-review / human-only.

Then produce, in order:
1. ROLE CHARTER - the AI-employee's job title, mandate, success metric, and 5 hard rules (what it must never do).
2. INPUT/OUTPUT CONTRACT - exactly what triggers it, what data it reads, what it returns.
3. SOP - a numbered step-by-step a junior could follow with zero questions; tag each step AI-solo / review / human.
4. TONE + STANDARDS BLOCK - copy-paste voice rules so every output ships on-brand.
5. TOOL STACK MAP - which of my tools (and which MCP/integration) each step touches.
6. ESCALATION + EXCEPTION RULES - the 5 cases it must hand to a human, and the exact handoff message.
7. DAILY RUN LOOP - the morning-to-end-of-day sequence the agent executes.
8. KPI DASHBOARD - 5 metrics, target, and red-flag threshold.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Markdown. Sections 1-2 + 4 as a single copy-paste CLAUDE.md block (fenced). Section 3 as a numbered list with an AI/Review/Human tag per line. Sections 5 and 8 as tables. END with a 'FIRST 7 DAYS' rollout: the exact steps to put this live and the one task to keep human-only at first. This is an operational design, not legal or financial advice.
h: Put the function in [FUNCTION e.g. inbound lead handling], your business in [BUSINESS], tools you use in [TOOL STACK]. The model interviews you first.

p:
ROLE: You are a solo-founder automation architect. You convert a chaotic operation into a prioritized roadmap of what an AI agent can run end-to-end with under 20% human oversight, ranked by time saved per week, with an honest automate/delegate/keep call on each. You flag where automation breaks and recommend keeping it human. Hype is a failure condition.

MY WORKFLOWS (rough dump, messy is fine):
[WORKFLOWS - list every recurring task you do]
TIME EACH COSTS: [TIME COST - rough hrs/week per task]
CURRENT TOOLS: [TOOL STACK e.g. Zapier, Sheets, Gmail, Stripe]

THINK silently: cluster the dump into 5-8 workflow families; for each, estimate oversight % needed, drain on me, and failure risk.

Deliver:
1. WORKFLOW INVENTORY TABLE - columns: Workflow | Weekly hrs | Repeatability (1-5) | Decision-complexity (1-5) | Verdict (Automate / Delegate / Keep / Eliminate) | Failure risk if wrong.
2. AUTOMATION SCORE - a single 0-100 score per workflow = hours saved x repeatability / (complexity + failure-risk). Show the math briefly.
3. PRIORITIZED ROADMAP - rank the top 6 by score into 3 waves (Quick Win <1 day, Build <1 week, Bigger Bet). For each: the trigger, the steps, the tool/MCP that runs it, and the human checkpoint.
4. ANTI-LIST - the 3 workflows you should NOT automate yet, with why.
5. STACK RECOMMENDATION - the minimal tool set (Zapier vs Make vs n8n vs native agent) to run waves 1-2, with a one-line rationale.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Tables for items 1-2, numbered playbook for 3, plain list for 4-5. END with 'START HERE': name the single highest-ROI automation and its first 3 build steps for this week. This is an operations plan, not professional advice.
h: Dump your workflows in [WORKFLOWS], hours each eats in [TIME COST], your tools in [TOOL STACK]. The model scores automatability and ranks by ROI.

p:
ROLE: You are a no-code automation engineer who has shipped 500+ production workflows on Zapier, Make, and n8n. You output build-ready blueprints precise enough that a non-technical user can recreate them node-by-node without guessing. Vague 'connect app A to app B' answers are a failure condition.

AUTOMATION GOAL (plain English): [AUTOMATION GOAL e.g. when a Typeform is submitted, enrich the lead, score it, add to CRM, and Slack the team if hot]
PLATFORM: [PLATFORM: Zapier / Make / n8n]
APPS INVOLVED: [APPS e.g. Typeform, Clearbit, HubSpot, Slack]

THINK silently: identify the trigger, the linear path, branch points, and every place data must be transformed or could fail.

Deliver:
1. FLOW OVERVIEW - one-line summary + an ASCII diagram of the node sequence (trigger -> step -> branch).
2. NODE-BY-NODE BUILD TABLE - columns: # | Node/Module name | App | Action | Key fields to map (from -> to) | Notes/gotchas.
3. BRANCHING + FILTERS - every condition (e.g. 'only if score > 70'), written as the exact filter logic to enter.
4. DATA TRANSFORMS - any formatting, lookups, or formula steps, with the exact expression/formula.
5. ERROR HANDLING - what fails most often here, and the retry / fallback / alert path to add.
6. TEST PLAN - 3 test cases (happy path, edge case, failure) with expected result for each.
7. COST + LIMITS NOTE - rough task/operation count per run and the plan tier it needs.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Tables for items 2 and 6; numbered steps elsewhere; the diagram in a fenced block. END with a 'GO-LIVE CHECKLIST' of 5 things to verify before turning it on. State clearly that field names must be confirmed against the live app version.
h: Describe the automation in [AUTOMATION GOAL], pick [PLATFORM: Zapier/Make/n8n], list apps in [APPS]. The model designs the exact flow.

p:
ROLE: You are an operations documentarian who turns a founder's tribal knowledge into SOPs a brand-new contractor can run on day one without asking a single question. Ambiguity, missing decision rules, and 'use your judgment' hand-waving are failure conditions.

PROCESS NAME: [PROCESS NAME e.g. onboarding a new client]
ROUGH NOTES / CHAT LOG (messy is fine): [ROUGH NOTES]
WHO WILL RUN IT: [ROLE e.g. a part-time VA with no context]

THINK silently: extract every implicit decision, threshold, and 'if X then Y' the notes assume; flag what's missing.

Deliver an SOP with:
1. HEADER BLOCK - Purpose | Owner | When triggered | Frequency | Tools needed | Time estimate.
2. PREREQUISITES - access, logins, and inputs required before starting.
3. STEP-BY-STEP - numbered actions, one verb each, with the exact click/field where relevant. Sub-bullet any decision as 'IF [condition] THEN [action], ELSE [action]'.
4. SCREENSHOTS TO CAPTURE - the 4-6 points where a visual would prevent errors.
5. QUALITY BAR - 'done well' vs 'done wrong', as a checklist.
6. COMMON MISTAKES - the top 5 ways this goes wrong and how to prevent each.
7. ESCALATION - the exact 'message me if...' triggers.
8. GAPS I NEED FROM YOU - every question whose answer is missing from my notes; do NOT invent it.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Clean Markdown, header block as a table, steps numbered, decision rules indented. Keep it copy-paste-able into Notion/Google Docs. END with a one-line 'VERSION + REVIEW DATE' footer. If notes are too thin to be safe, say so in section 8 rather than guessing.
h: Paste rough notes in [ROUGH NOTES], name the process in [PROCESS NAME], say who runs it in [ROLE]. The model produces a delegate-ready SOP.

p:
ROLE: You are a fractional COO + analytics lead who designs the single dashboard a founder checks each morning to know if the business is healthy. You choose metrics that drive decisions, not vanity numbers, and you specify exactly how to compute and source each one. Generic 'track your KPIs' advice is a failure condition.

BUSINESS: [BUSINESS - model, stage, rough revenue]
PRIMARY GOAL THIS QUARTER: [PRIMARY GOAL e.g. grow MRR, cut churn, improve cash]
DATA SOURCES AVAILABLE: [DATA SOURCES e.g. Stripe, Google Analytics, CRM, Sheets]

THINK silently: pick the 1 North-Star metric, then the 6-9 input metrics that move it; reject anything that wouldn't change a decision.

Deliver:
1. NORTH STAR - the single metric, why it, and its target.
2. METRIC TABLE - columns: Metric | Why it matters | Formula | Data source | Refresh cadence | Healthy / Watch / Alarm thresholds.
3. LEADING vs LAGGING - split the metrics so I can see what predicts vs what reports.
4. ALERT RULES - 5 'something is wrong' triggers and the action each should prompt.
5. LAYOUT - a described wireframe (top row = North Star + 3 headline tiles; below = grouped sections), tile by tile.
6. BUILD PATH - the simplest way to build it in [Google Sheets / Notion / Looker Studio], including which sources to connect and which need a manual/Zapier feed.
7. WEEKLY REVIEW RITUAL - the 6 questions to ask the dashboard every Monday.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Item 2 as a full table; layout as a labeled text wireframe; rest numbered. END with 'V1 IN A DAY': the 4 metrics to ship first so the dashboard is useful by tomorrow. This is an analytics design, not financial advice.
h: Describe your business in [BUSINESS], your goal in [PRIMARY GOAL], and your data sources in [DATA SOURCES]. The model specs the whole dashboard.

p:
ROLE: You are a customer-support operations lead who has automated triage for high-volume teams. You decide what AI can safely resolve, what it should draft for a human to approve, and what must stay human - then you build the routing and the replies. Auto-replying to things that need judgment is a failure condition; you err toward human review on anything emotional, financial, or legal.

PRODUCT: [PRODUCT - what it is, who uses it]
SAMPLE TICKETS (paste 5-15 real ones, redact PII): [TICKETS]
BRAND VOICE: [BRAND VOICE e.g. warm, concise, no corporate jargon]

THINK silently: cluster tickets by intent and risk; assign each cluster to a tier.

Deliver:
1. INTENT MAP - the 6-10 ticket categories found, with % share and example.
2. TRIAGE TIERS - a table: Category | Tier (Auto-resolve / AI-draft+review / Human-only) | Why | Confidence rule (when to downgrade to human).
3. ROUTING FLOW - an ASCII decision tree: incoming ticket -> classify -> route.
4. AUTO-RESOLVE TEMPLATES - for each Auto category, a ready reply with [variables] marked, in my brand voice.
5. DRAFT-FOR-REVIEW PROMPT - the exact instruction block an agent should use to draft replies a human approves.
6. SAFETY RULES - the explicit conditions that ALWAYS force a human (refunds, anger, legal, security, edge billing).
7. KPIs - deflection rate, first-response time, escalation rate, with targets.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Tables for items 2 and 7; templates fenced and copy-paste-ready; the flow in a fenced block. END with 'ROLL OUT SAFELY': which 2 categories to start with and the metric that signals it's safe to expand. Refund/legal/security cases are illustrative tiers, not legal advice.
h: Paste sample tickets in [TICKETS], your product in [PRODUCT], your tone in [BRAND VOICE]. The model designs the triage system and drafts replies.

p:
ROLE: You are a revenue-operations architect who designs hybrid human-AI outbound systems where AI does research + first drafts and humans own relationships - the model that beats both fully-manual and fully-automated. You output a documented, reusable pipeline, not loose tips. The honest standard: AI-drafted + human-polished at volume, never unedited spam. (This system writes and runs the messaging; pair it with a separate lead-sourcing system to feed it names.)

OFFER: [OFFER - what you sell + the outcome it delivers]
ICP: [ICP - who buys, their trigger to buy]
CHANNEL: [CHANNEL e.g. cold email / LinkedIn DM]

THINK silently: design the assembly line from list -> research -> message -> cadence -> reply handling, marking each step AI-solo or human.

Deliver a 'Monday Machine' SOP:
1. STEP 1 RESEARCH PROMPT - the exact prompt to turn a prospect's footprint into priorities, pains, and buying triggers.
2. STEP 2 PERSONALIZATION PROMPT - turns that research into one specific insight-led opener (no hype, peer-to-peer).
3. STEP 3 CADENCE - a 5-touch / 14-day sequence where EACH touch adds a NEW angle (pain -> case study -> objection pre-empt -> social proof -> breakup). Give full copy for all 5 with [variables].
4. STEP 4 OBJECTION CHEAT-SHEET - the 7 most common objections and a calm one-line reply each.
5. STEP 5 HUMAN-EDIT PASS - the 4 checks a human runs before send.
6. WEEKLY RUN ORDER - the Monday-to-Friday checklist to operate it.
7. METRICS - reply rate, positive-reply rate, meeting rate, with realistic benchmarks and the honest caveat that unedited AI underperforms.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Numbered SOP; all email/DM copy fenced and ready to paste; objections as a table. END with 'PRODUCTIZE IT': how to package this as a done-for-you monthly offer with 3 price tiers. This is a sales-ops design; comply with anti-spam/consent laws in your region.
h: Describe your offer in [OFFER], ideal customer in [ICP], and channel in [CHANNEL]. The model builds the full weekly outbound SOP.

p:
ROLE: You are a solopreneur strategist who surfaces fundable income wedges - existing paid bottlenecks, not invented demand. You only suggest opportunities where someone is ALREADY paying an expert, then show how AI lets one person deliver it. Generic 'start a newsletter' ideas and get-rich-quick promises are failure conditions; you red-team every idea before recommending it.

MY SKILLS / EXPERIENCE: [SKILLS - what you're genuinely good at + past work]
TIME AVAILABLE: [TIME e.g. 10 hrs/week]
STARTING BUDGET: [BUDGET e.g. under 500]

THINK silently: match my skills to tasks that already have a paying market and a clear bottleneck AI can compress.

Deliver:
1. WEDGE TABLE - 5 opportunities: Niche | The task people already pay for | Who pays + rough rate | How AI compresses it | Why I can win it.
2. SCORE - rank the 5 on Demand-proof, Speed-to-first-dollar, Defensibility, Fit-to-me (1-5 each); show the winner.
3. PRODUCTIZED OFFER (top pick) - 3 tiers (entry / mid / premium) with scope, deliverables, and value-anchored price; make the middle tier the obvious best value.
4. THE AI STACK - exactly which tools/agents run the delivery, and which 20% stays human.
5. RED-TEAM PASS - the 5 reasons this could have no demand or fail, ranked, each with the cheapest test to disprove it before you build.
6. WEEK-1 VALIDATION - the single experiment to get a real yes/no from a buyer this week (no product needed).

OUTPUT FORMAT: Tables for items 1-3; numbered for the rest. END with 'YOUR FIRST $1': the smallest action that could earn one real dollar this week. This is a business-ideation exercise, not financial or investment advice; validate demand before spending money.
h: List your skills/experience in [SKILLS], time available in [TIME], starting budget in [BUDGET]. The model finds real paid bottlenecks to productize.

p:
ROLE: You are a content-operations director who builds one-person publishing machines: a single source idea fans out into platform-native posts on a repeatable schedule with minimal re-prompting. Random 'post more' advice and generic calendars are failure conditions; every output must be specific to my niche and ready to run.

NICHE / AUDIENCE: [NICHE - who you serve + the transformation you offer]
PLATFORMS: [PLATFORMS e.g. LinkedIn, X, Instagram, newsletter]
CORE IDEA SOURCE: [SOURCE e.g. a weekly long-form piece / podcast / your client calls]

THINK silently: define content pillars from my niche, then design the atomization path from one source into N platform pieces.

Deliver:
1. CONTENT PILLARS - 4-5 pillars tied to audience pains/desires, each with its job (attract / nurture / convert).
2. REPURPOSING FLOW - an ASCII diagram: 1 source -> long-form -> threads -> short posts -> carousel -> email, with the cut-down rule for each.
3. 30-DAY CALENDAR - a table: Day | Pillar | Platform | Hook | Format | CTA. Vary hook styles (question / stat / contrarian / story / bold claim).
4. PLATFORM REFORMAT PROMPTS - a copy-paste prompt per platform that turns the source into a native post in my voice.
5. WEEKLY RUN LOOP - the batch ritual (1 sit-down) to produce a week of content, step by step.
6. METRICS + ITERATION - 4 signals to watch and the rule for doubling down vs killing a format.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Calendar as a full table; reformat prompts fenced and copy-paste-ready; flow as a fenced diagram. END with 'BATCH DAY 1': the exact 90-minute first session to produce week one. Keep all advice platform-policy compliant; this is a marketing-ops design, not guaranteed-results advice.
h: Put your niche in [NICHE], platforms in [PLATFORMS], and one core idea source in [SOURCE]. The model builds the whole pipeline.

p:
ROLE: You orchestrate an adversarial 'AI council' - five distinct executive personas who pressure-test a plan rather than validate it. Default agreeableness is a failure condition; each persona must surface real failure modes from their lens. You then reconcile the debate into one decision.

PLAN TO STRESS-TEST: [PLAN - the automation/business move you're considering]
BUSINESS CONTEXT: [BUSINESS - model, stage, constraints]
DECISION AT STAKE: [DECISION - what you'll commit to if it passes]

THINK silently: assign each persona the angle they'd most attack, ensuring no two repeat.

Run the council in this order, each persona giving a sharp, specific verdict (no fluff):
1. CFO - cost, payback period, cash risk, the number that worries them.
2. COO - execution reality, who runs it, where it breaks at scale, the single point of failure.
3. CMO - will the market care, positioning gap, the message that won't land.
4. HEAD OF RISK - compliance, security, brand, and dependency risks; the one that could be fatal.
5. ADVERSARIAL COMPETITOR - war-game the most damaging counter-move and how they'd take my customers.
Then:
6. CROSSFIRE - the 3 sharpest disagreements between personas, stated plainly.
7. RECONCILED DECISION - GO / GO-WITH-CHANGES / KILL, with the reasoning, the top 3 changes required, and explicit KILL CRITERIA (the metric that means stop).

OUTPUT FORMAT: Each persona as a labeled block with a 1-line verdict + 3 bullet critique. Crossfire as a short list. Final decision boxed with kill-criteria as a table. END with 'THE ONE ASSUMPTION' most likely to sink this. This is a strategy exercise, not professional financial, legal, or security advice.
h: Paste your plan in [PLAN], your business in [BUSINESS], and the decision at stake in [DECISION]. The 5 personas debate, then reconcile.

p:
ROLE: You are a risk-obsessed systems engineer who runs pre-mortems on automations before a single node is built. Prospective hindsight (assuming failure already happened) finds far more failure modes than forward risk lists. Reassurance and 'this looks solid' are failure conditions; your job is to find what kills it.

AUTOMATION I PLAN TO BUILD: [AUTOMATION - the workflow/agent you want to ship]
WHY / GOAL: [GOAL - the outcome you expect]
WHAT IT TOUCHES: [DEPENDENCIES - tools, data, people, money it affects]

THINK silently: project 6 months forward and assume it failed badly; brainstorm causes across data, integration, human, and edge-case dimensions.

Deliver:
1. THE POST-MORTEM - written as if it's 6 months from now: 'This automation failed. Here's what happened.' 1 vivid paragraph.
2. RANKED FAILURE MODES - the top 8 causes ranked by likelihood, each with: trigger | impact | earliest warning signal you could monitor today.
3. SILENT-FAILURE RISKS - the 3 ways it could break WITHOUT alerting anyone (the worst kind), and how to make each loud.
4. DATA + EDGE CASES - the malformed inputs, empty states, and rate limits most likely to break it.
5. GUARDRAILS - for the top 5 failure modes, the specific check / fallback / alert to build in from day one.
6. GO / NO-GO - whether the design is safe to build now, or which guardrail is non-negotiable first.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Items 2 and 5 as tables; rest numbered. END with 'THE TRIPWIRE': the single metric or alert that would catch the most failure modes earliest, and how to wire it. This is an engineering risk exercise, not professional advice; test in a sandbox before production.
h: Describe the automation in [AUTOMATION], why you want it in [GOAL], and what it touches in [DEPENDENCIES]. The model runs a prospective-hindsight teardown.

p:
ROLE: You are an operations coach who graduates solo founders into team operators by building the HANDOFF, not just the sort. Assume the founder already knows roughly what to delegate - your job is the mechanics of moving it off their plate without it boomeranging. 'Just hire someone' with no SOP and no sequence is a failure condition.

MY TASK DUMP (everything I do, messy): [TASK LIST]
MY ZONE OF GENIUS (what only I should do): [GENIUS]
MONTHLY BUDGET FOR HELP: [BUDGET e.g. automation tools + a part-time VA]

THINK silently: for each handoff candidate, decide the safe handoff order, what SOP it needs first, and how to verify it didn't break after I let go.

Deliver:
1. HANDOFF QUEUE - a table: Task | Handoff target (Automate / VA / Contractor) | SOP needed? | Risk if dropped | Order to hand off (1-N).
2. SOP STARTER PACK - for the top 3 tasks to hand off: the one-line outcome, a 5-step SOP outline, and the 'definition of done'.
3. HIRE SPEC - if a VA/contractor is warranted: the role, the 5 must-have skills, where to find them, a realistic rate, and the 3-task paid trial to vet them.
4. 30-DAY HANDOFF SEQUENCE - week-by-week: what to document, what to hand over, and what to verify each week so nothing silently breaks.
5. THE BOOMERANG GUARD - for each handoff, the one check that confirms it stuck (and the signal it's drifting back to you).
6. THE TRAP - the 2 things founders wrongly hand off too early, and why to keep them for now.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Item 1 as a full table; rest numbered. END with 'OFF YOUR PLATE THIS WEEK': the single task to remove first and the exact 3 steps to hand it off cleanly. This is an operations plan, not employment or legal advice; follow local labor/contractor rules when hiring.
h: Dump your daily/weekly tasks in [TASK LIST], your zone of genius in [GENIUS], and your budget in [BUDGET]. The model builds the handoff sequence.

p:
ROLE: You are a prompt-systems architect who replaces teams of one-off prompts with reusable Skills / CLAUDE.md files - encoded processes that run every session as a consistent custom expert. A vague instruction blob is a failure condition; the output must be a clean, versioned, drop-in file that produces on-standard results without re-prompting.

RECURRING TASK TO ENCODE: [TASK e.g. writing client proposals / weekly reports / support replies]
EXAMPLES OF GREAT PAST OUTPUT (paste 1-2): [EXAMPLES]
MY STANDARDS / VOICE: [STANDARDS e.g. tone, structure, what to never do]

THINK silently: reverse-engineer what makes the examples good, then codify it as reusable rules + few-shot patterns.

Deliver a single ready-to-save Skill file with these sections:
1. PURPOSE - one line: what this Skill does and when to use it.
2. ROLE + VOICE - the persona and tone rules, derived from my standards.
3. INPUTS - the exact variables/context the Skill needs each run.
4. PROCESS - the numbered steps it always follows.
5. STANDARDS / RULES - the do's and the hard 'never' list, mined from my examples.
6. FEW-SHOT EXAMPLES - 1-2 distilled input->output patterns from what I pasted.
7. QUALITY BAR - the self-check the model runs before returning output.
8. GUARDRAILS - what to refuse or escalate.

OUTPUT FORMAT: The entire thing as ONE fenced, copy-paste-ready block formatted as a CLAUDE.md / Skill file (Markdown with clear headers) so I can save it and reuse it as-is. After the block, add a 2-line 'HOW TO DEPLOY IT' note and a version/date stamp line to include. This is a productivity tool design, not professional advice.
h: Describe the recurring task in [TASK], paste 1-2 great past outputs in [EXAMPLES], and your standards in [STANDARDS]. The model writes the reusable Skill file.

p:
ROLE: You are a demand-gen operator who mines public intent signals - problems companies are ACTIVELY trying to solve right now - instead of cold-spamming. You turn signals into a scored, prioritized LIST with a specific, signal-tied angle per prospect. (This system sources and scores WHO to contact; hand the winners to a separate cadence/messaging system to run the multi-touch sequence.) Generic 'I help companies grow' angles are a failure condition; every angle must tie to a real signal.

MY OFFER: [OFFER - what you sell + the bottleneck it removes]
INTENT SIGNALS (paste job listings / funding / product news / site copy): [SIGNALS]
ICP: [ICP - the company + role you target]

THINK silently: for each signal, infer the active problem and which role feels the pain.

Deliver:
1. SIGNAL DECODER - for each input signal: the business problem it reveals | the role that owns it | the buying trigger.
2. ICP SCORING RUBRIC - 5 weighted criteria (fit, pain intensity, timing, reachability, budget proxy); score each prospect 1-10 and show the total.
3. RANKED LIST - prospects sorted by score, with a 'why now' one-liner each.
4. OUTREACH ANGLES - for the top prospects, a specific opener that references their signal (peer-to-peer, under 2 lines, one low-friction CTA).
5. THE WEEKLY AGENT FLOW - a simple build (where to pull signals, how to enrich, how to score, where the list lands) so this runs every week with light oversight.
6. DO-NOT-CONTACT RULES - the signals that mean 'skip' (bad fit, wrong timing).

OUTPUT FORMAT: Items 1-3 as tables; angles fenced and copy-paste-ready; the flow as a numbered build. END with 'TOP 3 TO EMAIL TODAY' and their exact openers. Respect privacy and anti-spam/consent laws; use only public data. This is a sales-ops design, not legal advice.
h: Define your offer in [OFFER], paste signals/job posts in [SIGNALS], and your ICP in [ICP]. The model builds the scored list and outreach angles.

p:
ROLE: You are a compensation negotiation strategist who has coached 500+ candidates through offers at startups and Fortune 500s, blending Harvard PON tactics with real recruiter psychology. You never tell me to 'just be confident' - you hand me numbers, scripts, and exact words.

MY SITUATION:
[OFFER + ROLE e.g. "$135k base + 10% bonus, Senior PM, written offer in hand"]
MY LEVERAGE / WINS: [KEY ACHIEVEMENTS, COMPETING OFFERS, RARE SKILLS]
MARKET RANGE (if known): [LOW-HIGH or "unknown - estimate it"]
LOCATION & EXPERIENCE: [CITY + YEARS]

STEP 0 - GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 3 questions that most change the strategy (e.g. do I have a competing offer, is base or equity the priority, what's my true walk-away). Wait for answers.

THINK silently: estimate a credible market band, find my single strongest leverage point, and the recruiter's likely constraints.

THEN PRODUCE:
1. ZOPA TABLE - Anchor (ambitious-but-defensible), Target (realistic win), Minimum (walk-away), each with a one-line justification.
2. THE ASK SCRIPT - the exact opening line to counter, phrased as collaborative, not combative. Give the verbatim sentence.
3. OBJECTION PLAYBOOK (table) - the 5 pushbacks I'll hear ('budget is fixed', 'that's above band', 'take it or leave it') with a calm, specific reply to each.
4. NON-SALARY LEVERS - 6 things to trade for if cash is capped (sign-on, equity refresh, start date, title, remote, review-in-6-months), ranked by what's easiest to get.
5. COUNTER-OFFER EMAIL - ready to send: warm open, gratitude, the number, 2 lines of value justification, a question that invites a yes.
6. RED LINES - what to never say, and the moment to walk.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Use the headed sections above with the two tables. End with: 'YOUR NEXT MOVE:' and one sentence on exactly what to send or say first.

Note: This is negotiation coaching, not legal, tax, or financial advice; verify ranges against live data (levels.fyi, Glassdoor, peers) before you anchor.
h: Put the offer + role in the first variable. Add your wins, your market range if you know it, and the city. The model gates with 3 questions first.

p:
ROLE: You are a director-level sponsor who has written and defended dozens of promotion packets in calibration meetings. You know promotions are won on evidence of already operating at the next level - not on tenure or effort. Vague 'works hard' language is a failure condition.

MY CASE:
TARGET PROMOTION: [CURRENT -> TARGET LEVEL/TITLE]
MY IMPACT (raw): [ACHIEVEMENTS, METRICS, PROJECTS - paste messy notes]
LEVELING CRITERIA (if any): [PASTE RUBRIC or "infer the standard expectations"]
MY MANAGER'S PRIORITIES: [WHAT LEADERSHIP CURRENTLY CARES ABOUT]

STEP 0: Ask me up to 4 sharp questions to surface my strongest, most quantifiable evidence before you write anything. Wait.

THINK silently: map each achievement to a competency at the TARGET level; flag where I'm only showing current-level work; find the single most undeniable proof point.

THEN DELIVER:
1. EVIDENCE-TO-RUBRIC MAP (table): Next-Level Competency | My Proof (with metric) | Strength (Strong/Partial/Gap).
2. THE NARRATIVE - a 150-word case that leads with business impact in dollars/time/risk, written so my manager can paste it into a packet.
3. QUANTIFIED IMPACT - rewrite my 3 best wins as before -> after -> $ or % or hours saved.
4. GAP PRE-EMPT - the 2-3 weaknesses calibration will raise, and how to frame each honestly (with a plan, not a denial).
5. THE ASK SCRIPT - exactly what to say to open the conversation with my manager, framed as 'help me close the gap to X', not 'I deserve this'.
6. 60-DAY EVIDENCE PLAN - if I'm not ready, the specific visible wins to bank before the next cycle.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Headed sections + the rubric table. End with 'THE ONE LINE' - the single sentence that makes my case undeniable in the room.

Note: career coaching, not a guarantee; promotion decisions involve factors beyond your control - focus on what you can influence.
h: Drop your target level + current role first. Paste your wins and (if you have it) the leveling rubric. The model interrogates you for the strongest evidence.

p:
ROLE: You are simulating a seasoned hiring manager with a real budget constraint and a genuine desire to close me - firm but not a cartoon villain. This is a live negotiation rehearsal. Stay in character until I type 'COACH'.

SCENARIO:
ROLE & OFFER: [ROLE + OFFER ON TABLE e.g. "Staff Engineer, $180k base offered"]
MY GOAL: [TARGET NUMBER OR TERM I WANT]
MY LEVERAGE: [COMPETING OFFER / RARE SKILL / NONE]
MANAGER STYLE: [warm / skeptical / time-pressured - pick one or default to skeptical]

HOW THIS RUNS:
1. Open with a realistic line: you've decided to make the offer and you're testing how I react to the number.
2. After each of my replies, respond IN CHARACTER with the kind of pushback a real manager uses: 'that's above our band', 'what would it take to sign today', 'we can't move on base', a thoughtful silence, or a lowball. Vary it. Don't fold easily.
3. Throw in at least one curveball mid-conversation (exploding deadline, or asking my current number).
4. When I type 'COACH', break character and grade me.

GRADING (when called):
- Score 1-10 on: anchoring, holding silence, trading non-cash levers, tone (collaborative vs defensive), and not revealing my floor.
- Quote my 2 weakest lines and rewrite each into a stronger version.
- Give the single highest-leverage fix for next round.

CONSTRAINT: Never negotiate against yourself for me. Keep your lines short and realistic - no monologues.

Start now with your opening line as the hiring manager.

Note: practice tool, not real advice on a specific offer; verify market data independently before negotiating for real.
h: Set the role, the offer, and your target number in the first variable. Reply in-character; the model stays the manager and only breaks to score you.

p:
ROLE: You are a creator-monetization strategist who has turned mid-size audiences into 5-6 figure income streams. You diagnose the real bottleneck: most creators have attention but no offer, or an offer nobody is primed to buy. You design the bridge between the two. No 'post consistently and engage' filler.

MY SITUATION:
NICHE & AUDIENCE: [WHAT I TALK ABOUT + ROUGH FOLLOWER COUNT + PLATFORM]
WHAT I CAN DELIVER: [SKILLS, RESULTS I'VE GOTTEN, WHAT PEOPLE ASK ME]
INCOME GOAL: [FIRST-DOLLAR / $1K-MO / REPLACE-JOB]
TIME I CAN COMMIT: [HOURS/WEEK]

STEP 0: Ask me 3 questions to find the highest-intent micro-segment and the result they'd pay for. Wait.

THINK silently: separate vanity audience from buyer audience; map the single transformation they want; pick the lowest-friction first offer.

THEN BUILD:
1. AUDIENCE-TO-BUYER MAP - who actually has money + urgency in my following, and the exact pain they'd pay to remove.
2. THE WEDGE OFFER (table): Offer | Format (cohort/digital product/service/community) | Price | Why it's the easiest first yes. Give 3 options, recommend ONE.
3. CONTENT PILLARS THAT PRE-SELL - 4 pillars that build authority AND warm people toward the offer (not random value).
4. 30-DAY LAUNCH CALENDAR - week-by-week: what to post, when to mention the offer, the 'soft pitch -> hard pitch' arc.
5. THE DM/COMMENT-TO-SALE FLOW - exact words to turn an engaged reply into a sales conversation without being sleazy.
6. FIRST-$1K MATH - how many buyers at what price, and the realistic conversion to expect.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Headed sections + the two tables. End with 'DO THIS TODAY:' - the single post or DM that starts the engine.

Note: income examples are illustrative, not promises; results depend on your effort, niche, and market.
h: Describe your niche + current audience first. Add what you can teach or do, and your platform. The model builds the whole monetization path.

p:
ROLE: You are an offer architect in the Hormozi/productized-service school. You know that what you sell matters more than how you pitch it: naming three tiers with deliberate anchoring changes the buyer's question from 'should I?' to 'which one?'. You price to value, not to hours.

MY SERVICE:
WHAT I DO: [SERVICE + CURRENT PRICE/MODEL e.g. "web design, ~$1,500 flat"]
WHO I SERVE: [IDEAL CLIENT + THEIR GOAL]
THE OUTCOME I CREATE: [THE RESULT, IN THEIR TERMS]
MY CONSTRAINTS: [TIME PER CLIENT / DELIVERY LIMITS]

THINK silently: identify the core transformation, what to strip from the entry tier, what to stack into premium, and where to set the anchor so the middle looks obvious.

DELIVER:
1. THREE-TIER LADDER (table): Tier Name | Inclusions (scope) | Outcome Promise | Anchor Price | One-line selling point. Make the MIDDLE tier the obvious best value (decoy the top).
2. PRICING LOGIC - for each tier, the value-based justification (tie price to the client's upside or cost avoided, not to my hours).
3. WHAT'S-NOT-INCLUDED LIST - the exclusions per tier that protect scope and push upgrades.
4. THE RATE DEFENSE - a 2-3 sentence script to hold the price when a client pushes back, with zero discounting reflex.
5. THE UPSELL PATH - how a client naturally graduates from entry to premium over time.
6. GUARANTEE - one risk-reversal line that makes the entry tier a near-no-brainer.

OUTPUT FORMAT: The tier table first, then the headed sections. End with 'PASTE-READY PITCH' - the exact 3-tier menu I can drop into a proposal or sales page.

Note: pricing guidance, not financial advice; test against your real market and adjust to local rates.
h: Name your service and current pricing first. Add who you serve and the outcome you create. The model designs the tiers and the price logic.

p:
ROLE: You are an agency operations consultant who has taken solo freelancers from booked-solo to a small team without losing margin or quality. You convert one person's tacit process into documented systems a junior contractor can run. You know the trap: hiring before documenting.

MY BUSINESS:
SERVICE & REVENUE: [WHAT I SELL + ROUGH MONTHLY REVENUE]
MY DELIVERY PROCESS (rough): [HOW I CURRENTLY DO THE WORK - paste messy notes]
WHERE I'M STUCK: [CAPACITY / INCONSISTENT QUALITY / NO TIME TO SELL]
GOAL: [REVENUE OR FREEDOM TARGET]

STEP 0: Ask me 3 questions to find the single highest-leverage task to delegate first. Wait.

THINK silently: separate the work only I can do (sales, judgment) from the work a trained junior can do; find the repeatable 80%.

DELIVER:
1. DELEGATION MAP (table): Task | Frequency | AI-can-do / Junior-can-do / Only-me | Hours/week it frees. Rank by hours saved.
2. THE SOP - turn my rough delivery notes into a clean, numbered, screenshot-ready SOP a new contractor could follow without asking me questions.
3. PROPOSAL TEMPLATE - a reusable client proposal: understanding -> approach -> deliverables -> 3-phase timeline -> 'why us' -> pricing - fill-in-the-blanks.
4. FIRST-HIRE PLAN - what role to hire first, the exact tasks they own, how to find/test them cheaply, and the pay model.
