Using the attached photo, create a professional corporate headshot of this pet as if it were a company executive. Keep the same animal, breed, fur color, markings and facial features clearly recognizable. Dress it in [OUTFIT] and place it against a softly blurred modern office or grey studio backdrop. Light it like a real LinkedIn headshot: soft front key light, gentle fill, catchlights in the eyes, shallow depth of field, dignified and slightly serious expression. Optionally add a small desk nameplate reading exactly "[JOB TITLE]" with crisp legible text. CONSTRAINTS: photoreal fur and fabric, the clothing must sit naturally on the animal's body, no extra limbs, no human face, keep it charming and believable rather than cartoonish, one animal only. OUTPUT: the edited image only.
h: Upload a clear photo of your pet, then set the [OUTFIT] (e.g. tiny grey suit and tie) and a [JOB TITLE] for an optional nameplate.

p: ROLE: You are a period-photography stylist and colorist. Using the attached photo, transport this person to the year [DECADE]. Keep the exact same face, identity and recognizable features, but restyle the hair, makeup and wardrobe to be authentically of that era, and place them in this setting: [SCENE]. THINK FIRST (silently): pick the era-correct film look before editing. Match the photographic look of the period: for the 70s/80s use warm grainy film with a slight color shift and soft focus; for the 90s use a flash-lit point-and-shoot snapshot look; for Y2K/early 2000s use a harsh on-camera flash, slight overexposure and digital-camera color. Add the appropriate grain, contrast and color cast so it reads as a real photo from that decade, not a clean modern edit. CONSTRAINTS: do not change face shape or make a different person, photoreal, era-correct props only, accurate hands, no modern phones or logos unless the decade allows. OUTPUT: the edited image only.
h: Upload a clear photo, then choose the [DECADE] (e.g. 1985) and an optional [SCENE] (e.g. mall arcade).

p: ROLE: You are a film-emulation specialist replicating a 2000s point-and-shoot. Using the attached photo, restyle it to look like an authentic early-2000s disposable-camera snapshot. Keep the same people, poses and setting recognizable. Apply: harsh direct on-camera flash with bright foreground and falloff into shadow, slight red-eye feel, visible film grain, mild motion blur, lowered dynamic range, a faint warm-green color cast and tiny dust specks. Add a thin white film border and, in the lower-right corner, a small orange-yellow digital date stamp reading exactly "[TIMESTAMP]". Optionally handwrite "[CAPTION]" in the white bottom border. Goal: a deliberately imperfect, nostalgic 'found photo' feel that fights the over-clean AI look. CONSTRAINTS: keep faces recognizable, do NOT beautify or smooth skin, no high-dynamic-range polish, legible date stamp, keep it grounded and real. OUTPUT: the edited image only.
h: Upload a photo, then set an optional [TIMESTAMP] (e.g. 06 22 2006) and a [CAPTION] for the white border.

p: ROLE: You are a precision retoucher doing a non-destructive local edit. Using the attached photo, make exactly ONE targeted change and nothing else: [ONE CHANGE]. Everything else must stay identical: [WHAT TO KEEP] — specifically keep the same face, expression, head pose, body position, framing, camera angle, lighting direction and color temperature, and keep the background unless the change explicitly involves it. THINK FIRST (silently): decide which pixels must move and which must be left untouched. Blend the edit so it matches the existing shadows, reflections and grain of the original, with realistic fabric folds, edges and contact shadows. Do not restyle the whole image, do not shift the crop, and do not subtly alter the person's identity. CONSTRAINTS: photoreal, seamless edge blending, preserve original resolution and detail, no added people or objects beyond the requested change. OUTPUT: the edited image only.
h: Upload a photo, then state the [ONE CHANGE] you want and the [WHAT TO KEEP] you must not alter.

p: ROLE: You are a character-consistency artist building a reusable storyboard character. Using the attached photo as the locked reference, generate a new image of this EXACT same person in a different scene. New scene: [NEW SCENE]. New pose: [NEW POSE]. New expression: [NEW EXPRESSION]. CRITICAL: preserve identity precisely — same facial structure, eye color, skin tone, hairstyle, age and the same outfit as the reference unless the scene requires a stated wardrobe change. The person must be instantly recognizable as the same character across this and any future images. Relight them naturally to fit the new environment (correct shadow direction, color spill and ambient tone) without changing who they are. CONSTRAINTS: photoreal, accurate hands and proportions, no identity drift, no face morphing toward a generic model, one person only, match the realism level of the reference. OUTPUT: the edited image only, ready to reuse as the next storyboard frame.
h: Upload a clear reference photo, then describe the [NEW SCENE], an optional [NEW POSE] and [NEW EXPRESSION].

p: ROLE: You are a commercial product photographer building a storefront hero image. Using the attached photo of my product, turn it into a premium studio hero shot for an online store. Keep the exact same product shape, color and proportions. Place it on/in this setting: [SCENE], with clean professional lighting — a soft key light, a subtle rim light to define edges, a gentle gradient backdrop, and a realistic contact shadow and reflection. On the product's label/packaging, render the brand name exactly as "[BRAND NAME]" and a small tagline exactly as "[LABEL TAGLINE]" in crisp, legible, well-aligned typography that wraps naturally to the surface curvature. CONSTRAINTS: photoreal materials (glass, plastic, metal or paper as appropriate), text sharp and free of gibberish, do not distort the product silhouette, keep one product as the hero, leave clean negative space suitable for a storefront. OUTPUT: the edited image only.
h: Upload a photo of your product, then set the [BRAND NAME], a short [LABEL TAGLINE] and a [SCENE] (e.g. marble countertop).

p: ROLE: You are a cinematographer lighting a noir film still. Using the attached photo, restyle this into a cinematic neo-noir portrait. Setting: [SETTING]. Keep the same face, identity and recognizable features intact. Relight the scene dramatically with deep shadows and [NEON COLORS] neon glow reflecting on skin, glass and wet surfaces. Add a rain-streaked window or atmosphere, soft bokeh from distant city lights, a teal-and-amber cinematic color grade, gentle film grain and a shallow depth of field that isolates the subject. Make the mood pensive and filmic, like a still from a modern noir thriller. CONSTRAINTS: photoreal, do not alter face shape or identity, realistic reflections and rain physics, keep skin texture natural under the neon, no text, single subject, preserve the original head pose and crop where possible. OUTPUT: the edited image only.
h: Upload a clear photo, then set the [NEON COLORS] (e.g. cyan and magenta) and a [SETTING] (e.g. inside a car at night).

p: ROLE: You are a graphic designer producing a share-ready infographic. Using the attached photo as the central image, design a clean, modern step-by-step infographic card. THINK FIRST (silently): plan the grid, text hierarchy and where the hero image anchors the layout. Render the heading at the top reading exactly "[HEADING]" in bold large type. Below it, lay out these numbered steps as a tidy vertical or grid list, each with a small icon and short label, in this order: [STEPS]. Use this brand palette: [BRAND PALETTE] for accents, headings and background, with high-contrast readable body text. Keep the uploaded subject as the focal hero image, integrated with consistent lighting. CRITICAL: every word of text must be spelled correctly, sharp and legible with proper alignment, kerning and spacing — no gibberish or warped letters, no lorem-ipsum, no stray characters. Make it feel like a polished Pinterest/Instagram infographic with clear hierarchy and generous spacing. CONSTRAINTS: balanced composition, consistent icon style, one cohesive color system. OUTPUT: the infographic image only.
h: Upload the subject photo, then set the [HEADING], the [STEPS] (semicolon-separated) and a [BRAND PALETTE] of two hex colors.

p: ROLE: You are a museum-grade photo restorer. Using the attached old photo, restore and gently colorize it while staying faithful to the original people and scene. Repair scratches, creases, dust, stains and torn edges; reduce noise; and recover natural sharpness and detail in faces, hair and fabric. If the image is black-and-white or faded, add realistic period-appropriate color: natural skin tones, believable clothing and background colors. Honor these known details where given: [KNOWN DETAILS]. CRITICAL: do NOT change anyone's identity, facial features, age, expression or pose — keep every face true to the original. Do not add or remove people or objects, and do not over-smooth skin into a plastic look; preserve authentic texture and the original's character. CONSTRAINTS: photoreal, historically plausible colors, keep the original composition and crop, natural film-like finish. OUTPUT: the restored image only.
h: Upload the old or damaged photo, then optionally note [KNOWN DETAILS] (e.g. her dress was deep red).

p: ROLE: You are a toy designer mocking up a packaged vinyl collectible. Using the attached photo, create a stylized vinyl collectible toy of this person in the popular big-head, small-body chibi style. Keep recognizable cues — same hairstyle, hair color, skin tone, glasses, outfit colors and any signature features — translated into smooth matte vinyl with simplified shapes and large round eyes. Place the figure inside a cardboard window box: a clear plastic front panel, a colored cardback, the name "[YOUR NAME]" printed cleanly across the top, and a small molded [SIGNATURE ACCESSORY] beside the figure. Style: studio product photography, soft even lighting, slight top-down angle, clean background. CONSTRAINTS: smooth toy-like vinyl finish (not photoreal skin), legible box text with no gibberish, correct proportions for the chibi style, one figure only, friendly collectible vibe. OUTPUT: the edited image only.
h: Upload a clear photo, then set [YOUR NAME] for the box and a [SIGNATURE ACCESSORY] that represents you.

p: You are a typographic poster designer who writes Ideogram V3 prompts where TEXT is the hero and never misspells. Build ONE shareable quote card. Exact headline text (render verbatim, inside double quotes so Ideogram treats it as literal type): "[QUOTE]". Credit line (verbatim): "[ATTRIBUTION]". Vibe: [VIBE]. THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): which 1-2 words to emphasize, how many lines the quote breaks across, and whether a large high-contrast display serif or a clean grotesque sans suits the mood. Then write a SINGLE Ideogram prompt that specifies: layout (centered stacked text, generous margins, clear size hierarchy between quote and credit), a named typeface feel, exact case (Title Case or ALL CAPS), a 2-3 color palette as explicit hex codes, and a subtle uncluttered background behind the words. Append Ideogram parameters on the same line: 'Style: Design, Aspect ratio: 4:5, Magic Prompt: Off' (Magic Prompt OFF so the literal text is preserved). RULES: text must be spelled exactly as given with correct punctuation and apostrophes; no extra invented words; no duplicate or mirrored text; no watermark; legible at thumbnail size. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line, exact text in double quotes, params appended> 2) PALETTE: <three hex codes + what each is for> 3) ONE TWEAK: <a single word to swap for a different mood>. Output nothing else.
h: Put the exact line in [QUOTE], the credit in [ATTRIBUTION], and an optional [VIBE] (minimalist, bold, vintage).

p: You are a YouTube thumbnail designer who writes Ideogram V3 prompts engineered for click-through, where the overlay text is huge, legible on a phone, and spelled exactly. Topic of the video: [TOPIC]. Render this EXACT overlay text (verbatim, in double quotes, keep it 2-4 words): "[HOOK TEXT]". Emotion to provoke: [EMOTION]. Accent color (optional hex): [ACCENT COLOR]. THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): where to place the text block so it never covers a face, which word gets the biggest weight, and the single bold contrast device (thick stroke, glow, or color block) that keeps text readable over any background. Then write ONE Ideogram prompt specifying: a punchy 16:9 composition with the hook text occupying one third of the frame in an extra-bold condensed sans with a heavy outline or color-block backing, an open area reserved for a person or subject, high-saturation contrast, dramatic lighting matched to [EMOTION], and the accent color (or a high-contrast default) as explicit hex. Append parameters on the same line: 'Style: Design, Aspect ratio: 16:9, Magic Prompt: Off'. RULES: render the hook EXACTLY with correct casing; no extra micro-text or gibberish labels; keep the text to a few words so it stays sharp at small sizes; no watermark. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line, hook in double quotes, params appended> 2) FACE ZONE: <one line on where to leave space for the subject> 3) CTR TIP: <one line on the contrast device used>. Output nothing else.
h: Set the [HOOK TEXT] (2-4 words), the [TOPIC], an [EMOTION] (shock, hype, curiosity), and an optional [ACCENT COLOR].

p: Act as a senior brand identity designer using Ideogram V3 for typographic logo concepts where the lettering must be perfect. Brand name to render EXACTLY (in double quotes): "[BRAND NAME]" - it does [WHAT IT DOES]. Optional tagline to render below (verbatim): "[TAGLINE]". Style: [STYLE]. THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): what association the mark must trigger, whether a pure wordmark or a lettermark plus a small icon reads best, and which letterforms to feature. Then write ONE Ideogram prompt that specifies: a centered wordmark logo on a clean solid background, a named type personality (geometric sans, humanist serif, monospaced techy, etc.), precise letter spacing, an optional small symbol that does NOT replace any letters, the tagline in smaller tracked caps beneath, a 2-color palette as explicit hex codes, and a flat vector feel with no gradients and no photorealism. Append parameters on the same line: 'Style: Design, Aspect ratio: 1:1, Magic Prompt: Off'. RULES: spell the brand name and tagline letter-for-letter with no extra characters; no fake registration symbols unless asked; no busy texture; the mark must still work in one flat color. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line, exact text in double quotes, params appended> 2) PALETTE: <two hex codes + roles> 3) SCALABILITY NOTE: <one line on keeping the name legible when shrunk>. Output nothing else.
h: Give [BRAND NAME], [WHAT IT DOES], an optional [TAGLINE], and a [STYLE] (modern, vintage, techy, playful).

p: You are a gig-poster designer who writes Ideogram V3 prompts with multi-line, perfectly legible text. Build ONE event poster. Render these EXACT strings (verbatim, each in double quotes): headline "[EVENT TITLE]", details line "[DATE & VENUE]", optional subline "[SUBLINE]". Style: [POSTER STYLE] (e.g. 70s screenprint, brutalist type, neon synthwave, swiss minimalist). THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): the visual hierarchy order (title biggest, then subline, then date/venue), where to place each block, and which display font matches the era. Then write ONE Ideogram prompt specifying: a poster composition with clear typographic hierarchy and breathing room, the named font/era feel, exact casing for each line, a 2-3 color palette as explicit hex codes true to the style, and a simple supporting graphic that never overlaps the text. Append parameters on the same line: 'Style: Design, Aspect ratio: 2:3, Magic Prompt: Off'. RULES: every string spelled exactly with correct dates and punctuation; no duplicated or garbled text; no random extra words; high legibility. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line, all three strings in double quotes, params appended> 2) HIERARCHY: <order of text sizes> 3) PALETTE: <hex codes + roles>. Output nothing else.
h: Set [EVENT TITLE], the [DATE & VENUE] line, an optional [SUBLINE], and a [POSTER STYLE].

p: You are a packaging designer using Ideogram V3 to mock up front-of-pack typography that reads perfectly. Product type: [PRODUCT TYPE]. Render these EXACT strings on the pack (verbatim, each in double quotes): brand "[BRAND]", product name "[PRODUCT NAME]", key claim "[KEY CLAIM]". THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): the label hierarchy (brand vs product vs claim), where each sits on the pack face, and a palette that signals the product category. Then write ONE Ideogram prompt specifying: a clean studio product shot of the [PRODUCT TYPE] with a printed label, the three strings placed in clear hierarchy with correct spelling and casing, a named type style, a 2-3 color palette as explicit hex codes, soft front lighting, and a neutral seamless background. Append parameters on the same line: 'Style: Realistic, Aspect ratio: 4:5, Magic Prompt: Off' (Realistic for the product, Magic Prompt off so label text stays literal). RULES: spell every string exactly; keep claim text short and legible; no gibberish ingredient lists or fake fine print; no warped label; no second product. This is a design concept only, not a real health, nutrition or safety claim - keep wording generic. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line, the three strings in double quotes, params appended> 2) PALETTE: <hex codes + roles> 3) LEGIBILITY TIP: <one line on keeping small label text crisp>. Output nothing else.
h: Give [BRAND], the [PRODUCT NAME], a [KEY CLAIM] line, and the [PRODUCT TYPE] (box, pouch, can, jar, bottle).

p: You are a print-and-poster typographer writing Ideogram V3 prompts for framed wall art. Render this EXACT phrase as the centerpiece (verbatim, in double quotes): "[PHRASE]". Aesthetic: [AESTHETIC]. Orientation: [ORIENTATION] (portrait, landscape, or square). THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): how the phrase breaks across lines for rhythm, which word gets the largest weight, and the type/illustration style that fits [AESTHETIC]. Then write ONE Ideogram prompt specifying: a bold typographic composition with the phrase as the dominant element, deliberate line breaks, a named display type feel, supporting geometric or illustrative shapes true to the aesthetic, a 2-4 color palette as explicit hex codes, and generous margins for framing. Append parameters on the same line: 'Style: Design, Magic Prompt: Off' followed by the matching aspect ratio for [ORIENTATION] ('Aspect ratio: 2:3' for portrait, '3:2' for landscape, or '1:1' for square). RULES: spell the phrase exactly with correct punctuation; no extra invented words; no watermark; print-quality and high legibility. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line, exact phrase in double quotes, params appended> 2) LINE BREAKS: <show how the phrase wraps> 3) PALETTE: <hex codes + roles>. Output nothing else.
h: Put your phrase in [PHRASE], pick an [AESTHETIC] (bauhaus, art-deco, grunge, pastel), and an [ORIENTATION].

p: You are an infographic designer using Ideogram V3 to render clean, legible step cards. Build ONE numbered how-to infographic. Render the EXACT heading (in double quotes): "[HEADING]". Render these EXACT short steps as a numbered list, each verbatim: [STEPS]. Brand palette (optional hex codes): [PALETTE]. THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): how many steps fit cleanly, the grid layout (single vertical list or 2-column), and how to keep every step line short enough to render crisply. Then write ONE Ideogram prompt specifying: a tidy infographic layout with a bold heading at the top, numbered step rows each with a small simple icon and a short label, consistent spacing, a named clean sans for body text, the supplied palette (or a tasteful default expressed as explicit hex codes), and a flat modern style. Append parameters on the same line: 'Style: Design, Aspect ratio: 4:5, Magic Prompt: Off'. RULES: spell the heading and every step exactly; keep each step to a few words so text stays sharp; correct, sequential numbers; no garbled micro-text; no watermark. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line, heading and steps in double quotes, params appended> 2) STEP COUNT: <how many steps and the layout used> 3) LEGIBILITY TIP: <one line on keeping step text crisp>. Output nothing else.
h: Give the [HEADING], a list of [STEPS] (short lines), and an optional brand [PALETTE] as hex codes.

p: You are a greeting-card illustrator writing Ideogram V3 prompts where hand-lettering must spell correctly. Occasion: [OCCASION]. Render the EXACT front greeting (verbatim, in double quotes): "[FRONT GREETING]". Optional smaller inside-style line (verbatim): "[INSIDE LINE]". Style: [STYLE]. THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): whether script, brush or rounded sans hand-lettering best matches the occasion and style, and how illustrative elements can frame the words without crowding them. Then write ONE Ideogram prompt specifying: a charming card front with the greeting as hand-lettered focal text, decorative illustration that borders rather than overlaps the text, exact spelling and casing, a soft cohesive palette as explicit hex codes, and a warm balanced lighting feel. Append parameters on the same line: 'Style: Design, Aspect ratio: 2:3, Magic Prompt: Off'. RULES: spell the greeting and inside line exactly with correct punctuation; keep lettering legible (script must remain readable); no gibberish flourishes that look like extra letters; no watermark. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line, exact text in double quotes, params appended> 2) PALETTE: <hex codes + roles> 3) LETTERING TIP: <one line on keeping script readable>. Output nothing else.
h: Set the [OCCASION], the [FRONT GREETING], an optional [INSIDE LINE], and a [STYLE] (florals, retro, kids, elegant).

p: You are a book-cover art director using Ideogram V3 where the typography must spell correctly and signal the genre. Render these EXACT strings (verbatim, each in double quotes): title "[TITLE]", optional subtitle "[SUBTITLE]", author "[AUTHOR NAME]". Genre/mood: [GENRE/MOOD]. THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): the cover hierarchy (title dominant, author smaller, subtitle supporting), the type style and color language readers expect for this genre, and a central image or texture that supports rather than fights the text. Then write ONE Ideogram prompt specifying: a portrait book-cover composition with the title as the largest element, the author name placed conventionally (top or bottom), the subtitle in smaller tracked type, a named typeface feel matched to genre, a 2-3 color palette as explicit hex codes, and a tasteful background motif. Append parameters on the same line: 'Style: Design, Aspect ratio: 2:3, Magic Prompt: Off'. RULES: spell every string exactly with correct casing; no duplicate title text; no garbled author name; legible at thumbnail size; no watermark. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line, all strings in double quotes, params appended> 2) HIERARCHY: <size order of the three text blocks> 3) PALETTE: <hex codes + roles>. Output nothing else.
h: Give [TITLE], optional [SUBTITLE], [AUTHOR NAME], and the [GENRE/MOOD] (thriller, self-help, fantasy, business).

p: You are a conversion-focused marketing designer using Ideogram V3 for promo banners with crisp, on-brand text. Render these EXACT strings (verbatim, each in double quotes): headline "[HEADLINE]", offer "[OFFER]", call-to-action button "[CTA]". Brand palette (optional hex): [PALETTE]. THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): the read order a scrolling viewer follows (headline, then the offer as the boldest element, then the CTA), and how to keep all three legible against any background. Then write ONE Ideogram prompt specifying: a clean promotional banner layout, the offer as the largest punchy element, a clearly styled CTA that looks like a tappable button, a named bold sans, the supplied palette (or a high-contrast default expressed as explicit hex codes), and a simple supporting graphic or product silhouette that never covers the text. Append parameters on the same line: 'Style: Design, Aspect ratio: 16:9, Magic Prompt: Off' (swap to '1:1' for a square feed banner). RULES: spell every string exactly including the percent or number in the offer; keep copy short; no gibberish fine print; high contrast for legibility; no watermark. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line, all strings in double quotes, params appended> 2) READ ORDER: <how the eye should travel> 3) PALETTE: <hex codes + roles>. Output nothing else.
h: Set the [HEADLINE], the [OFFER] (e.g. 30% OFF), the [CTA] button text, and an optional brand [PALETTE].

p: You are an editor who writes natural-language image-edit prompts for text-capable models (Ideogram remix/edit) that render legible text on an EXISTING uploaded photo. Goal: keep the uploaded image, subject and face exactly as-is, and overlay a caption that spells correctly. Render this EXACT caption (verbatim, in double quotes): "[CAPTION]". Placement: [PLACEMENT]. Text style: [TEXT STYLE] (e.g. bold sans lower-third, handwritten marker, vintage serif title, neon sign). THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): where the caption sits without covering the subject's face, and whether it needs a subtle band, shadow or outline to stay readable over the photo. Then write ONE conversational prompt that: explicitly says to preserve the same photo, composition, face and lighting; describes the caption text, its exact placement and the named text style; and specifies a contrast treatment (drop shadow, semi-transparent bar, or stroke) so the words stay crisp. End the line with 'keep everything else unchanged, Magic Prompt: Off' so the edit stays literal and the caption is preserved. RULES: render the caption EXACTLY with correct spelling, casing and punctuation; do not alter or beautify the subject's face; no extra text; no watermark. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste conversational line, caption in double quotes, instruction appended> 2) CONTRAST TIP: <one line on keeping text readable over the photo> 3) FALLBACK: <one alternate placement if the caption clashes with the subject>. Output nothing else.
h: Upload a photo, set the [CAPTION] text, the [PLACEMENT] (top, bottom, corner), and a [TEXT STYLE].

p: You are a typographic artist using Ideogram V3 to make a single-word art print where imagery is built into the letters. Render this EXACT word as the entire composition (verbatim, in double quotes): "[WORD]". Concept to express through the letterforms: [CONCEPT]. Art style: [ART STYLE]. THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): how the chosen concept can fill or decorate each letter (negative space, scenes inside the strokes, textures) while keeping the word instantly readable. Then write ONE Ideogram prompt specifying: large display letterforms spelling the word, the [CONCEPT] integrated INTO the letters (not separate clip-art beside them), the named art style and rendering, a cohesive palette as explicit hex codes, a clean background that lets the word pop, and generous margins for a framed print. Append parameters on the same line: 'Style: Design, Aspect ratio: 1:1, Magic Prompt: Off' (swap to '2:3' for a portrait print). RULES: the word must spell exactly and stay clearly legible despite the decoration; no extra letters or duplicate words; one word only; no watermark. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line, the word in double quotes, params appended> 2) LETTER IDEA: <one line on how the concept fills the letters> 3) PALETTE: <hex codes + roles>. Output nothing else.
h: Put one word in [WORD], the [CONCEPT] to weave into the letters, and an [ART STYLE] (line art, watercolor, 3D, retro).

p: You are a senior brand-identity designer who writes image-model prompts. Brand: [BRAND] - [WHAT IT DOES]. Desired vibe: [VIBE]. Color steer / things to avoid: [COLOR OR AVOID]. THINK FIRST (silently): the single association the mark must trigger, and which shapes/letterforms/colors earn it; reject cliche symbols (globe, swoosh, lightbulb) unless truly apt. Produce FIVE distinct logo routes: (A) clean wordmark, (B) lettermark/monogram, (C) abstract geometric symbol, (D) emblem/badge, (E) symbol+wordmark combo. For EACH route write ONE copy-paste prompt that specifies: flat vector logo, minimal, geometric construction, a named 2-3 colour palette WITH hex codes, even negative space, solid white background, centered, generous margin, scalable, no gradients, no photorealism, no 3D, no mockup, no text artifacts. OUTPUT FORMAT (Markdown table, columns: Route | Prompt | Midjourney params | Why it fits). Use these MJ params per route: '--ar 1:1 --style raw --no photo, realistic shadow, 3d, gradient'. After the table add: PICK: one line naming the strongest route for [VIBE] and why. Nothing else.
h: Give [BRAND] + [WHAT IT DOES], a [VIBE] (e.g. premium fintech, playful pet brand), and an optional [COLOR/AVOID].

p: Act as a logotype and monogram specialist. Build ONE master image prompt for a geometric monogram using the initials [INITIALS] for a [INDUSTRY] brand, in a [STYLE] direction. THINK FIRST (silently): how the letters can interlock, share a stroke, or create a hidden shape in the negative space; ensure the letters stay READABLE at favicon size. The prompt must specify: single flat vector monogram, built on a visible-then-removed geometric grid (golden ratio or modular), consistent stroke weight, balanced counters, elegant negative space, ONE accent colour on white (give a hex pair), no gradient, no bevel, no drop shadow, no 3D, centered with margin, transparent-ready. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line>. 2) MIDJOURNEY VERSION: same line ending '--ar 1:1 --style raw --s 80 --no shadow, gradient, 3d, photo'. 3) IDEOGRAM/DALL-E NOTE: <one line on rendering the letters cleanly>. 4) READABILITY CHECK: <one line on how to confirm it works at 32px>. Nothing else.
h: Set [INITIALS] (1-3 letters), the [INDUSTRY], and a [STYLE] (e.g. luxury serif, techy sans, hand-drawn).

p: You are a mascot and character-logo designer. Brand: [BRAND + AUDIENCE]. Base character: [ANIMAL OR CHARACTER TYPE]. Personality: [PERSONALITY]. THINK FIRST (silently): one memorable silhouette and one signature feature that makes it instantly ownable and trademark-distinct; keep it simple enough to reproduce as a flat logo. Deliver in this OUTPUT FORMAT: 1) LOCKED IDENTITY BLOCK: a plain-text line fixing species/type, body proportions (chibi vs sleek), exact colour palette with hex codes, signature accessory, and expression - this stays byte-for-byte identical across renders. 2) MASTER LOGO PROMPT: flat vector mascot, bold clean outlines, limited palette, friendly expression, solid white background, centered, scalable, no gradient mesh, no photoreal fur, no 3D render; append '--ar 1:1 --style raw --no photo, 3d, realistic'. 3) THREE POSE PROMPTS: waving, thinking, celebrating - each PREPENDS the identity block verbatim so the mascot looks identical. 4) CONSISTENCY NOTE: one line on using --cref / a reference image to lock the face. Nothing else.
h: Describe the [BRAND + AUDIENCE], pick an [ANIMAL/CHARACTER TYPE], and a [PERSONALITY] (e.g. cheeky, wise, energetic).

p: You are an app-icon designer who knows iOS/Android icon grids and the 'one idea, one shape' rule. App: [APP NAME + WHAT IT DOES]. Core metaphor to explore: [CORE METAPHOR]. Platform: [PLATFORM]. THINK FIRST (silently): reduce the brand to ONE bold glyph centered on the icon keyline grid; ensure it reads as a 1-bit silhouette and survives at 16px; avoid tiny details, text, and thin lines. The prompt must specify: single app icon, centered bold glyph, flat or subtle single-direction gradient duotone, rounded-square safe area with padding, vibrant 2-colour palette WITH hex, crisp edges, solid background, no text, no photoreal, no skeuomorphic clutter, no drop shadow. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) ICON PROMPT: <one copy-paste line>. 2) MIDJOURNEY: same line + '--ar 1:1 --style raw --s 120 --no text, photo, 3d clutter'. 3) SCALE TEST: <one line on checking it at 16/32/180px>. 4) LIGHT/DARK NOTE: <one line on a background swap for both modes>. Nothing else.
h: Give [APP NAME + WHAT IT DOES], a [CORE METAPHOR] you like, and the [PLATFORM] (iOS, Android, web).

p: Act as a brand strategist + art director shipping a coherent starter identity. Brand: [BRAND + WHAT IT DOES]. Customer: [TARGET CUSTOMER]. Positioning: [POSITIONING]. THINK FIRST (silently): the ONE strategic idea every visual choice must ladder up to, and reject any element that does not reinforce it. OUTPUT FORMAT - return these labelled sections only: 1) BRAND IDEA: one sentence. 2) PALETTE: primary, secondary, neutral, accent - each with name, exact hex, and a use note; confirm body text vs background passes WCAG AA contrast. 3) TYPOGRAPHY: a display + body pairing (Google Fonts only) with a one-line rationale and fallback web-safe stack. 4) LOGO CONCEPT: 2-3 sentence direction naming the mark type (wordmark / symbol+wordmark / monogram). 5) MOTIF/PATTERN: one repeatable graphic device derived from the logo. 6) BRAND VOICE: 3 adjectives + one do/don't line. 7) IMAGE PROMPTS: (a) a flat-vector LOGO prompt and (b) a seamless brand-PATTERN prompt - both specifying flat vector, the exact palette hexes, solid white background, even negative space, no gradient, no photoreal, no 3D, append '--ar 1:1 --style raw'. Reuse identical hex values across every section - no drift. Nothing else.
h: Describe [BRAND + WHAT IT DOES], the [TARGET CUSTOMER], and a [POSITIONING] (e.g. premium-minimal, bold-disruptor).

p: You are a logo designer famous for negative-space and dual-meaning marks. Brand: [BRAND + WHAT IT DOES]. The visible shape should be [SHAPE A]; the hidden shape revealed in the negative space should be [SHAPE B]. THINK FIRST (silently): how the gap between elements forms [SHAPE B] WITHOUT being forced; both readings must be clean and instantly legible in a single colour. The prompt must specify: minimalist flat vector logo, clever negative-space concept where the empty space forms [SHAPE B], single solid colour (give a hex), perfectly balanced, geometric, solid white background, centered, no gradient, no shadow, no 3D, no text, scalable to one colour. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) CONCEPT: one line explaining the hidden idea. 2) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line>. 3) MIDJOURNEY: same line + '--ar 1:1 --style raw --s 60 --no gradient, shadow, 3d, text, photo'. 4) FALLBACK: one line on how to simplify if the hidden shape reads as noise. Nothing else.
h: Give [BRAND + WHAT IT DOES] and two concepts to fuse: [SHAPE A] hiding [SHAPE B] in the negative space.

p: Act as a personal-branding designer. Person: [FULL NAME], known for [NICHE]. Desired feel: [FEEL]. THINK FIRST (silently): which of three formats suits this person best - (a) initials monogram, (b) stylised signature, (c) clean name wordmark - and pick the strongest, but provide all three. For EACH format write ONE image prompt specifying: flat vector personal logo, refined letterforms, balanced spacing, single accent colour on white WITH a hex pair, minimal, no gradient, no photoreal, no 3D, centered with margin, scalable to a profile-pic circle. OUTPUT FORMAT (Markdown table: Format | Prompt | Midjourney params | Best used for). MJ params: '--ar 1:1 --style raw --no shadow, 3d, photo, busy detail'. After the table: RECOMMEND one format for [NICHE] in a single line, and add a PFP TIP line on cropping it into a circular avatar. Nothing else.
h: Give [FULL NAME], what you're known for in [NICHE], and a [FEEL] (e.g. editorial, techy, handcrafted).

p: You are a no-nonsense art director reviewing a logo. Logo: [DESCRIPTION OR UPLOAD]. Brand context: [BRAND CONTEXT]. Where it feels off: [WHAT FEELS WRONG]. Be brutally honest and specific - do not flatter. THINK FIRST (silently): judge it against scalability, legibility at small size, uniqueness, balance, colour, timelessness, and concept. OUTPUT FORMAT - return: 1) SCORECARD: a table scoring each of those 7 criteria /10 with a one-line reason. 2) TOP 3 PROBLEMS: ranked, each with the fix. 3) KEEP: what is actually working (be fair). 4) REDESIGN BRIEF: a tight paragraph an image model can use. 5) FIXED PROMPT: one copy-paste flat-vector prompt implementing your advice, with hex palette, white background, no gradient/3d/photo, ending '--ar 1:1 --style raw'. Note: this is design feedback, not a legal trademark clearance - tell me to run a separate trademark search before using any mark commercially. Nothing else.
h: Paste a [DESCRIPTION OR UPLOAD] of the logo, the [BRAND CONTEXT], and where it [FAILS/FEELS OFF].

p: You are a merch and sticker designer. Brand: [BRAND + VIBE]. Palette: [PALETTE OR HEX]. Motifs to include: [MOTIFS]. THINK FIRST (silently): how to keep 4-6 small designs visually unified by colour, line weight, and a thick white die-cut border, while each works alone. The prompt must specify: a cohesive sheet of die-cut vinyl stickers, flat bold vector style, thick white kiss-cut outline around each, consistent limited palette WITH hex, clean shapes, no fine gradients, no photoreal, on a neutral flat-lay background, even lighting, top-down. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) STICKER LIST: 4-6 sticker ideas in one line each. 2) SHEET PROMPT: <one copy-paste line for the full sheet>. 3) SINGLE-STICKER PROMPT: <one line for the hero sticker alone>. 4) MIDJOURNEY: append '--ar 4:5 --style raw --no busy gradient, photo realism, thin lines'. 5) PRINT TIP: one line on bleed/safe-zone for print-on-demand. Nothing else.
h: Give [BRAND + VIBE], your [PALETTE/HEX], and 3-5 [MOTIFS] you want on the stickers.

p: Act as a brand-systems designer producing a logo usage page for a style guide. Logo: [LOGO - symbol + name]. Brand colours: [BRAND COLORS OR HEX]. Primary use: [PRIMARY USE]. THINK FIRST (silently): which lockups this brand actually needs for [PRIMARY USE], and the minimum-size + clear-space rules that protect the mark from crowding and distortion. OUTPUT FORMAT - return labelled sections only: 1) LOCKUPS: define horizontal, stacked/vertical, icon-only, and one-colour (mono) versions, each in one sentence with its intended placement. 2) CLEAR SPACE: a rule expressed in units of the mark (e.g. 'minimum padding = the height of the symbol on all sides'). 3) MIN SIZE: smallest legible pixel/mm size for icon-only vs full lockup, for screen and print. 4) COLOUR VARIANTS: full-colour, all-black, all-white (knockout/reversed) - each with exact hex and the background it sits on. 5) MISUSE: 5 do-not rules (no stretch/distort, no recolour, no drop shadow, no busy/low-contrast background, no rotation). 6) IMAGE PROMPT: one flat-vector prompt that lays out all lockups and the clear-space diagram on a clean labelled grid board, solid white background, no gradient/3d/photo, ending '--ar 16:9 --style raw'. Nothing else.
h: Describe your [LOGO] (symbol + name), the [BRAND COLORS/HEX], and where it'll appear most: [PRIMARY USE].

p: You are an esports logo illustrator. Team/channel: [TEAM OR CHANNEL NAME]. Mascot: [MASCOT]. Vibe: [VIBE]. THINK FIRST (silently): one fierce, recognizable mascot head or crest with a strong silhouette that reads as a tiny circular avatar AND scales onto a jersey; avoid muddy detail. The prompt must specify: bold esports mascot logo, aggressive dynamic angle, thick clean black outlines, cel-shaded with sharp highlights, vivid 3-colour palette WITH hex, optional banner for the name, solid contrasting background, centered, high contrast, vector-style, no photoreal, no soft blur, no messy gradients. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line>. 2) MIDJOURNEY: same line + '--ar 1:1 --style raw --s 250 --no blur, photo, soft gradient, low contrast'. 3) AVATAR CROP: one line on the circular Twitch/Discord crop. 4) MONO VERSION: one line on a single-colour jersey-embroidery variant. Nothing else.
h: Give [TEAM/CHANNEL NAME], a [MASCOT] (animal/creature), and a [VIBE] (e.g. neon-cyber, gritty, regal).

p: You are an editing-focused image-model operator (Nano Banana / FLUX Kontext style). Base logo: [CURRENT LOGO]. Themes to generate: [NEW THEMES]. THINK FIRST (silently): the surgical-edit rule - change ONLY the requested theme element and explicitly preserve the mark's shape, proportions, letterforms, and composition. For EACH theme write one conversational edit prompt in this pattern: 'Restyle this logo as [THEME] - change [colour/texture/ornament] while keeping the exact same shape, proportions, letterforms, and layout; keep it flat vector and legible at small size.' Quote any literal text to keep with double quotes. OUTPUT FORMAT (Markdown table: Theme | Edit prompt | What to preserve | What changes). Add a final row 'Midjourney/MJ note' explaining to use '--sref [logo url] --sw 150 --ar 1:1 --style raw' instead, since MJ can't edit-in-place. Then one CAUTION line: keep edits subtle so brand recognition is preserved. Nothing else.
h: Upload/describe the [CURRENT LOGO], then list the [NEW THEMES] you want (e.g. holiday, monochrome, retro).

p: Act as a brand naming strategist who thinks in pictures. Product: [WHAT YOU SELL]. Audience + vibe: [AUDIENCE + VIBE]. Avoid: [MUST-AVOID]. THINK FIRST (silently): generate names that are short, pronounceable, .com-plausible, and ALREADY suggest a visual mark (a hidden object, a letter quirk, a metaphor). OUTPUT FORMAT - a Markdown table with columns: Name | Type (coined / compound / real-word / metaphor) | Why it works | Built-in logo idea | Logo image prompt. Give 8 names. Each 'Logo image prompt' must be a flat-vector, single copy-paste line with a hex palette, white background, no gradient/3d/photo, ending '--ar 1:1 --style raw'. After the table: TOP PICK - name the strongest name+mark pairing for [AUDIENCE + VIBE] and why, in one line. Add a NOTE: I have not checked trademark or domain availability - run a separate trademark and domain search before committing. Nothing else.
h: Describe [WHAT YOU SELL], the [AUDIENCE + VIBE], and any [MUST-AVOID] words or rivals.

p: You are a fashion-magazine cinematographer who writes Midjourney v6/v7 prompts. Turn my idea into ONE photoreal editorial beauty portrait. Person: [PERSON]. Mood: [MOOD]. Palette: [PALETTE]. THINK FIRST (silently, never shown): where the key light sits, what lens flatters the face, what single emotion the frame holds, what kills the 'too-perfect AI' look. Then write ONE copy-paste line of cinematic, comma-separated descriptors in this order: subject + expression, wardrobe + styling, environment, lighting (name the source + color temperature in K, e.g. soft Rembrandt key 5600K), lens + camera (e.g. 85mm f/1.4, shallow depth of field), color grade, skin and detail cues (visible pores, fine flyaway hairs, natural asymmetry), mood. HARD RULES: keep real skin texture, no waxy smoothing, no extra fingers, no text, no logos. End the line with realistic params: --ar 4:5 --v 6 --style raw --s 250. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) TWO TWEAKS: <two single-word swaps that shift the mood>. Nothing else.
h: Describe the [PERSON] in plain terms; set a [MOOD] and an optional [PALETTE].

p: You are a commercial product photographer who writes Midjourney v6/v7 prompts. Build ONE photoreal hero shot that an ad team could ship. Product: [PRODUCT]. Setting/surface: [SURFACE/SETTING]. Keep this [SIDE] of the frame as clean negative space for a headline. THINK FIRST (silently): hero angle, reflection vs matte, what light shape sells the material, where copy will sit. Then write ONE copy-paste line of cinematic, comma-separated descriptors: product + material + finish, placement on surface, studio environment, lighting (name it: softbox key + rim, gradient sweep, controlled specular highlights), camera (e.g. 100mm macro, f/8 tack sharp), reflections + shadow style, deliberate empty space on the [SIDE], premium mood. HARD RULES: accurate product shape, no warped text on the product, no clutter on the copy side, no second product. End with realistic params: --ar 16:9 --v 6 --style raw --s 150. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) LAYOUT NOTE: <one line on where headline + CTA go>. Nothing else.
h: Name the [PRODUCT], the [SURFACE/SETTING], and which [SIDE] should stay empty for text.

p: You are a feature-film cinematographer writing Midjourney v6/v7 prompts. Turn my idea into ONE photoreal cinematic still. Moment: [MOMENT]. Vibe: [DIRECTOR OR ERA]. Aspect ratio: [ASPECT RATIO]. THINK FIRST (silently): who the subject is, what dramatic beat is frozen, light direction + color, what lens sells the emotion, what atmosphere adds depth. Name a real director's cinematography instead of the word 'cinematic'. Then write ONE copy-paste line of comma-separated descriptors in order: subject + action, location + time of day, lighting (source + color temp in K, motivated practicals), film stock or lens (e.g. 35mm anamorphic, halation, gentle grain), color grade (e.g. teal-orange, bleach bypass), atmosphere (haze, rain, dust, god rays), composition + camera angle, mood. HARD RULES: no captions or subtitles, no extra people unless stated, no oversaturation. End with realistic params: --ar [ASPECT RATIO] --v 6 --style raw --s 250. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) MOOD SWAPS: <two single-word swaps>. Nothing else.
h: Give the [MOMENT] in a line, a [DIRECTOR OR ERA] vibe, and an [ASPECT RATIO].

p: You are an art director building a repeatable Midjourney v6/v7 brand system. Brand: [BRAND]. Vibe: [VIBE]. Scenes I need on-brand: [SCENES]. THINK FIRST (silently): which visual traits MUST stay fixed (palette, light, texture, mood) versus what changes per scene, so the whole feed looks like one hand made it. Deliver in this exact OUTPUT FORMAT: 1) STYLE DNA: one plain-text line of comma-separated descriptors fixing palette (name hex-ish colors), lighting character, texture/grain, lens feel, and overall mood in concrete words (ban 'beautiful', 'amazing'). 2) THREE SCENE PROMPTS: for each scene, one copy-paste line that PREPENDS the Style DNA verbatim, then adds the scene-specific subject + environment, each ending with realistic params: --ar 4:5 --v 6 --style raw --s 180 --sref [PASTE YOUR REFERENCE IMAGE URL HERE]. 3) CONSISTENCY NOTE: one line on reusing the same --sref and adjusting --sw for tighter or looser adherence. RULE: the Style DNA wording must be byte-for-byte identical in every scene prompt. Nothing else.
h: Describe the [BRAND] and its [VIBE]; name three [SCENES] you want on-brand.

p: You are a surrealist concept artist writing Midjourney v6/v7 prompts. Fuse two unrelated worlds into ONE impossible, beautiful scene people will save. World A: [WORLD A]. World B: [WORLD B]. Mood: [MOOD]. THINK FIRST (silently): the single arresting visual where the two worlds meet, the scale contrast, the light that makes it feel real not goofy, the one detail that sells the dream logic. Then write ONE copy-paste line of cinematic, comma-separated descriptors: the central fused subject, the impossible juxtaposition described literally, environment + scale cues, lighting (volumetric, rim, or bioluminescent as fits), atmosphere (mist, particles, refraction), camera + lens, art direction (matte-painting realism, Beeple-grade detail), mood. HARD RULES: keep it photoreal and grounded (real shadows, real physics of light), no melty AI mush, no text. End with realistic params: --ar 16:9 --v 6 --style raw --s 400 --c 15. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) WILD CARD: <one swap that makes it even stranger>. Nothing else.
h: Name two things to collide in [WORLD A] and [WORLD B]; add an optional [MOOD].

p: You are a corporate portrait photographer writing Midjourney v6/v7 prompts. Using my uploaded photo, produce ONE clean, professional headshot of THIS EXACT PERSON. Backdrop: [BACKDROP]. Wardrobe: [WARDROBE]. KEEP IDENTITY: same face shape, age, skin tone, hairline and distinguishing features, natural expression with a subtle confident smile. THINK FIRST (silently): flattering crop, key-light position, how to look polished without changing who they are. Then write ONE copy-paste line of comma-separated descriptors: professional headshot of the same person, [WARDROBE], [BACKDROP], lighting (soft three-point, gentle catchlights, 5500K), camera (85mm f/2.0, shallow depth of field), retouching cues (clean but real skin, no plastic smoothing), framing (head-and-shoulders, eye-level), confident mood. HARD RULES: do not alter identity, age or face shape; no extra people; natural hands if visible. End with realistic params: --ar 4:5 --v 6 --style raw --s 120 --cref [PASTE YOUR PHOTO URL] --cw 100. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) LIKENESS TIP: <one line on tuning --cw for stronger or looser face match>. Nothing else.
h: Upload your photo; pick a [BACKDROP] and a [WARDROBE] level (e.g. smart casual, suit).

p: You are a character designer building a reusable Midjourney v7 character (Omni Reference). Character: [CHARACTER]. Scenes: [SCENES]. THINK FIRST (silently): which traits MUST stay fixed across renders versus what may change per scene. Deliver in this exact OUTPUT FORMAT: 1) LOCKED IDENTITY BLOCK: one plain-text line of comma-separated descriptors fixing facial structure, apparent age, skin tone, eye color, hairstyle, body type and signature wardrobe in concrete adjectives (ban 'pretty', 'beautiful'). 2) THREE SCENE PROMPTS: for each scene, one copy-paste line that PREPENDS the Locked Identity Block verbatim, then adds the scene-specific environment, lighting and camera as cinematic comma-separated descriptors, each ending with realistic params: --ar 16:9 --v 7 --style raw --s 200 --oref [PASTE CHARACTER IMAGE URL] --ow 200. 3) CONSISTENCY NOTE: one line on lowering --ow when the face is fighting the scene, and that --oref is v7 only. RULE: the identity wording must be byte-for-byte identical in every scene. Nothing else.
h: Describe the [CHARACTER] once; name three [SCENES] to drop them into.

p: You are a retro portrait photographer writing Midjourney v6/v7 prompts. Using my uploaded photo, render THIS EXACT PERSON as an authentic portrait from the [DECADE]. Setting: [SETTING]. KEEP IDENTITY: same face, age and features; only the era changes. THINK FIRST (silently): the period-correct film stock, hairstyle, wardrobe, color cast and grain that scream that decade without caricature. Then write ONE copy-paste line of cinematic, comma-separated descriptors: period portrait of the same person, era-accurate hairstyle + wardrobe, [SETTING], film stock + process (e.g. Kodak Portra for 70s warmth, harsh direct flash + feathered hair for 80s, low-fi point-and-shoot for Y2K), lighting, slight grain + light leaks + period color cast, framing, nostalgic mood. HARD RULES: preserve identity, no modern items, no text, no over-clean look (it should read as a real old photo). End with realistic params: --ar 4:5 --v 6 --style raw --s 150 --cref [PASTE YOUR PHOTO URL] --cw 90. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) ERA SWAP: <one line giving the alt-decade descriptor set>. Nothing else.
h: Upload your photo; choose a [DECADE] and an optional [SETTING].

p: You are a surface-pattern designer writing Midjourney v6/v7 prompts. Create ONE seamless, print-ready repeat. Motif: [MOTIF]. Style: [STYLE]. Palette: [PALETTE]. THINK FIRST (silently): motif scale and spacing so it reads at small size, color balance, and how to keep edges tiling cleanly. Then write ONE copy-paste line of comma-separated descriptors: seamless repeating pattern of [MOTIF], [STYLE], evenly distributed balanced composition, [PALETTE], flat even lighting, crisp vector-clean edges, consistent scale, no focal subject, suitable for fabric and packaging. HARD RULES: edges must tile, no text, no watermark, no 3D perspective, no single hero object. End with realistic params: --tile --ar 1:1 --v 6 --style raw --s 100. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) USE NOTE: <one line on scaling the repeat for wrapping paper vs. a phone case>. Nothing else.
h: Name the [MOTIF], a [STYLE] (e.g. art deco, botanical), and a [PALETTE].

p: You are a food photographer writing Midjourney v6/v7 prompts. Build ONE appetizing hero shot a restaurant could put on a menu. Dish: [DISH]. Style: [STYLE]. Prop: [PROP]. THINK FIRST (silently): the angle that shows the food best (45deg vs top-down vs eye-level), backlight to make it glisten, garnish and steam for freshness, props that set the scene without crowding it. Then write ONE copy-paste line of cinematic, comma-separated descriptors: the dish plated, fresh garnish + texture detail, [STYLE] table styling with [PROP], background context, lighting (soft backlight + reflector fill to make food glisten, gentle steam, 5200K), camera (50mm or 100mm macro, shallow depth of field, 45-degree angle), shallow crumbs/condensation realism, warm appetizing mood. HARD RULES: photoreal food, accurate portions, no plastic-looking gloss, no text, no extra plates crowding the frame. End with realistic params: --ar 4:5 --v 6 --style raw --s 200. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) APPETITE TWEAK: <one line on adding steam, droplets or a sauce drizzle>. Nothing else.
h: Name the [DISH], a [STYLE] (rustic, fine-dining, street), and an optional [PROP].

p: You are an architectural photographer writing Midjourney v6/v7 prompts. Build ONE magazine-spread interior or exterior render. Space: [SPACE]. Style: [STYLE]. Light: [TIME OF DAY]. THINK FIRST (silently): the camera height + lens that keep verticals straight, how natural light enters, the few materials that define the style, and the styling that makes it feel lived-in not staged-dead. Then write ONE copy-paste line of cinematic, comma-separated descriptors: the space, [STYLE] design language, key materials (e.g. warm oak, travertine, brushed brass, linen), furniture + styling details, [TIME OF DAY] natural light pouring in with soft shadows, camera (16-24mm architectural lens, straight verticals, eye-level), color grade, calm aspirational mood. HARD RULES: correct perspective and straight lines, realistic materials, no warped geometry, no text, no people unless stated. End with realistic params: --ar 3:2 --v 6 --style raw --s 150. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) LIGHT SWAP: <one line giving a golden-hour vs overcast alt>. Nothing else.
h: Describe the [SPACE], a design [STYLE], and the [TIME OF DAY] light you want.

p: You are a toy-product photographer writing Midjourney v6/v7 prompts. Using my uploaded photo, render a hyper-real 3D collectible ACTION FIGURE of this exact person, face and hairstyle clearly recognizable, sealed in retail blister-pack packaging. THINK FIRST (silently): which accessories read at small scale, how to keep the cardback text clean. Then write ONE copy-paste line of comma-separated descriptors: photoreal collectible action figure of the same person, sealed clear plastic bubble on a printed cardback, bold header reading '[YOUR NAME]', subtitle reading '[TAGLINE]', three themed accessories in molded slots ([3 ACCESSORIES]), studio product photography, soft front lighting + slight top-down angle, clean seamless background, sharp plastic + cardboard texture. HARD RULES: accurate fingers, no warped or gibberish text, no second figure. End with realistic params: --ar 4:5 --v 6 --style raw --s 200 --cref [PASTE YOUR PHOTO URL] --cw 100. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) TEXT TIP: <one line on keeping the header and subtitle crisp>. Nothing else.
h: Upload a clear face photo; set [YOUR NAME], a [TAGLINE], and [3 ACCESSORIES].

p: You are a neo-noir cinematographer writing Midjourney v6/v7 prompts. Build ONE moody, screenshot-ready car portrait. Subject: [SUBJECT]. Neon/city mood: [CITY/NEON COLOR]. THINK FIRST (silently): the single shadowed angle through a rain-streaked window, where the neon catches the face, and what's deliberately left in darkness. Then write ONE copy-paste line of cinematic, comma-separated descriptors: [SUBJECT] seen through a rain-streaked car window, half-lit face, [CITY/NEON COLOR] neon reflections streaking across wet glass, deep shadow on the unlit side, rim light catching the jawline, shallow depth of field, 50mm lens, anamorphic flares, fine grain, moody contemplative atmosphere, color grade (cyan-magenta noir). HARD RULES: photoreal, keep deep blacks (no flat washed-out shadows), no text, no extra passengers unless stated. End with realistic params: --ar 16:9 --v 6 --style raw --s 300 --c 10. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) PALETTE SWAP: <one line giving an alt neon color scheme>. Nothing else.
h: Say who is in the [SUBJECT] seat and pick a [CITY/NEON COLOR] mood.

p: You are a Midjourney v6/v7 prompt engineer. Upgrade my weak prompt into a premium one. Rough prompt: [ROUGH PROMPT]. Goal: [GOAL]. THINK FIRST (silently): what subject the user actually wants, what's vague or missing (lighting, lens, environment, mood, params), and which 'hype words' to delete because v6/v7 weight information over adjectives. Reorder into cinematic comma-separated descriptors: subject + action, environment + time of day, lighting (named source + color temp), lens/camera + film feel, color grade, atmosphere, mood. Pick a sensible flag stack and explain it. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) DIAGNOSIS: 2-3 bullets naming what was missing or hurting the result. 2) UPGRADED PROMPT: <one copy-paste line of ordered descriptors ending with realistic params, e.g. --ar 16:9 --v 6 --style raw --s 250>. 3) FLAG NOTES: one line each explaining the chosen --ar, --s, --style raw, and any --sref/--cref/--c so I can tune them. 4) ONE VARIANT: a second descriptor set that changes only the mood. Nothing else.
h: Paste your [ROUGH PROMPT]; optionally state the [GOAL] image you actually want.

p: ROLE: You are a magazine-cover portrait photographer who writes surgically precise image-model prompts. TASK: Using the uploaded photo as the single source of identity, build a hyper-real 85mm editorial HEADSHOT of this exact person, keeping their face, skin tone, hairline, eye color and bone structure unmistakably recognizable. Vibe/role: [VIBE/ROLE]. Wardrobe: [WARDROBE]. Backdrop: [BACKDROP COLOR]. THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): where the key light sits, what separates real skin from plastic, which crop flatters this face, how to keep identity locked. SKIN + DETAIL CONSTRAINTS: keep visible pores, fine vellus hair, natural specular highlights and subtle subsurface glow; ENFORCE eyes tack-sharp with catchlights; BAN airbrushed/waxy/over-smoothed skin, extra or fused fingers, warped ears, mangled teeth, duplicated faces. OUTPUT FORMAT (print exactly these three labeled blocks, nothing before or after): 1) NANO-BANANA / DALL-E PROMPT: one conversational paragraph naming an 85mm f/1.8 look, a 3-light studio setup (key + soft rim + low fill), catchlights at 10 o'clock, shallow depth of field, and the literal line 'keep the same face and identity, photo-realistic not illustrated'. 2) MIDJOURNEY V7: one line ordered Subject > Wardrobe > Lighting > Lens, ending exactly with --ar 4:5 --style raw --s 120 --hd. 3) FLUX KONTEXT EDIT: one 'change only X, preserve the same facial features, expression and composition' instruction. Output nothing else.

You are an SDXL product photographer building a hero shot for e-commerce. Product: [PRODUCT]. Background: [BACKGROUND]. Mood/palette: [MOOD/PALETTE]. THINK SILENTLY: the lighting rig that flatters this material (matte vs glossy vs glass), reflections, and the negatives that kill warped logos and gibberish text. Build ONE comma-tag prompt: product as hero subject, centered, on [BACKGROUND], studio product photography, three-point softbox lighting, soft reflections, gentle shadow, shallow depth of field, [MOOD/PALETTE] color grade, then camera tags (macro lens, f/8, tack sharp) and boosters (product photography:1.2), (high detail:1.15), commercial quality, 8k, clean. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly:
Prompt: <one comma-tag line>
Negative prompt: <blurry, lowres, warped logo, gibberish text, distorted shape, harsh reflections, clutter, dust, fingerprints, deformed, watermark, jpeg artifacts, busy background>
Settings: Steps 30, CFG 5, Sampler DPM++ 2M Karras, 1024x1024, Hires fix.
Layout tip: <one line on using ControlNet/Image Guidance to lock the product silhouette>.
Nothing else.
h: Describe the [PRODUCT]; set a [BACKGROUND] (e.g. seamless white, marble) and optional [MOOD/PALETTE].

p:
You are a creature concept artist writing SDXL prompts for original, non-trademarked designs. Concept: [CREATURE CONCEPT]. Biome: [BIOME]. Render style: [RENDER STYLE]. THINK SILENTLY: believable anatomy for the biome (camouflage, locomotion, defense), a clear silhouette, and one memorable feature. Build ONE comma-tag prompt: original creature design, [CREATURE CONCEPT], anatomically plausible, distinctive silhouette, biome-adapted features for [BIOME], full-body 3/4 view, neutral concept-art lighting, render style tags ([RENDER STYLE], concept art, creature design, trending on artstation, intricate detail), then boosters (highly detailed:1.2), sharp focus, 8k. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly:
Prompt: <one comma-tag line>
Negative prompt: <deformed anatomy, extra limbs, fused limbs, blurry, low detail, copyrighted character, mascot, cartoon when realism wanted, text, watermark, signature, ugly>
Settings: Steps 35, CFG 6, Sampler DPM++ 3M SDE, 1024x1024, Hires fix 1.5x.
Design note: <one line naming the single feature that makes it memorable>.
Nothing else.
h: Set the [CREATURE CONCEPT] (e.g. glass-winged tundra wolf) and a [BIOME]; optional [RENDER STYLE].

p:
You are an SDXL art director building a poster, knowing SDXL renders dense text poorly. Headline: "[HEADLINE]". Theme/scene: [THEME/SCENE]. Palette: [PALETTE]. THINK SILENTLY: leave deliberate negative space for the headline, drive composition with tags, and plan the text as a separate pass. Build ONE comma-tag prompt: poster key art, [THEME/SCENE], strong focal subject, deliberate empty space for a headline, dynamic composition, [PALETTE] color scheme, dramatic lighting, then style + boosters (poster art:1.15), cinematic, highly detailed, sharp focus, 4k. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly:
Prompt: <one comma-tag line>
Negative prompt: <gibberish text, misspelled words, warped letters, cluttered, busy, low contrast, blurry, deformed, watermark, jpeg artifacts, no space for text>
Settings: Steps 32, CFG 6, Sampler DPM++ 2M Karras, 832x1216 (portrait poster).
Text plan: 1) generate the art with empty space, 2) inpaint or overlay "[HEADLINE]" in a real font in your editor (SDXL alone garbles long text), 3) optional: route the typography to Ideogram/Nano Banana for accuracy.
Nothing else.
h: Give the [HEADLINE] (exact words in quotes), the [THEME/SCENE], and an optional [PALETTE] in hex.

p:
You are an SDXL troubleshooting expert (A1111/ComfyUI, Juggernaut & community checkpoints). Current prompt: [PROMPT]. Problem: [WHAT WENT WRONG]. THINK SILENTLY: map each symptom to a real cause - melted hands (anatomy + steps), plastic skin (over-weighting + CFG), ignored subject (token order or over-long prompt), blur (Hires/denoise), washed color (CFG/sampler). Then fix it. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly:
Diagnosis: <2-4 bullets mapping symptom to cause>
Rebuilt prompt: <one comma-tag line, key subject moved earlier, emphasis weights trimmed to only what matters, quality boosters at the end>
Negative prompt: <a targeted negative that directly counters [WHAT WENT WRONG], e.g. deformed hands, extra fingers, mutated hands, plastic skin, oversaturated, plus standard lowres, blurry, bad anatomy, text, watermark>
Settings to change: <Steps, CFG, Sampler, resolution, Hires fix / denoise - state the new value and why>
Quick test: <one A/B change to confirm the fix>.
Nothing else. Note: tag-emphasis and CFG advice is model-version dependent - verify on your checkpoint.
h: Paste your current [PROMPT] and describe [WHAT WENT WRONG] (e.g. melted hands, plastic skin, ignored subject).

p:
You are an SDXL prompt consultant who refuses to guess. Rough idea: [IMAGE IDEA]. STEP 1 - INTERVIEW: ask me 5-7 diagnostic questions ONE AT A TIME (do not write the prompt yet), and for each propose the answer you'd recommend so I can just say 'yes, go with that'. Cover: realism vs anime vs concept art, checkpoint (Juggernaut XL / Animagine / Pony / other), subject details, mood + lighting, aspect ratio, and whether this is txt2img or img2img from a reference. STEP 2 - after I answer (or say 'use your picks'), produce the final SDXL recipe. OUTPUT FORMAT for the final answer exactly:
Prompt: <one comma-tag line, structured subject-to-boosters, emphasis weights only where needed>
Negative prompt: <a negative tuned to the chosen style and checkpoint>
Settings: <Steps, CFG, Sampler, resolution, Clip skip, Hires/denoise tuned to the checkpoint>
Why these choices: <2-3 bullets>
One variation: <a single token swap to try next>.
Do not output the final block until the interview is done or I say to use your picks. Nothing else.
h: Start with a rough [IMAGE IDEA]; answer the questions; it returns the prompt + negative + settings.

p:
You are a registered-dietitian-minded meal planner and frugal home cook. Build a 7-day dinner plan for [DIETARY NEEDS / GOAL], a weekly grocery budget of [BUDGET], cooking for [NUMBER OF PEOPLE], with a max of [MINUTES] minutes per meal on weeknights.

Rules: never repeat a main dish; deliberately reuse perishable ingredients across meals to cut waste; balance protein/veg/carbs each day; flag anything that batch-cooks or freezes well.

Think step by step before answering: (a) pick the 7 mains, (b) cross-check ingredient overlap and adjust to share perishables, (c) total the estimated cost and trim if over budget.

Output in this exact order: (1) a day-by-day table — Day | Meal | Prep time | Est. cost; (2) a grocery list grouped by store section (Produce, Protein, Pantry, Dairy/Frozen) with quantities; (3) one 'use-it-up' tip for likely leftovers; (4) total estimated cost vs. my budget, with the gap stated plainly.

This is general guidance, not medical or nutritional advice.
h: Fill [DIETARY NEEDS] (e.g. vegetarian, 1800 cal), then your budget and weeknight time limit.

p:
Act as a local-savvy travel planner who hates tourist traps and over-stuffed schedules. Plan a [NUMBER OF DAYS]-day trip to [DESTINATION] for a [TRAVEL STYLE] traveler (e.g. slow/foodie/adventure/family) on a [BUDGET LEVEL] budget.

Constraints: group activities by neighborhood to minimize backtracking; never schedule more than 3 anchor activities per day; include one rest/buffer block daily; flag anything needing advance booking.

Think first: cluster sights geographically, then sequence days to flow logically, then sanity-check pace.

For each day output: morning / afternoon / evening blocks, one signature meal recommendation with a price tier, walking-or-transit notes between stops, and a 'skip this if short on time' line.

End with: a packing-essentials shortlist for this destination/season, 3 local etiquette or safety tips, and a rough per-day cost estimate. Verify opening days/seasonality where you can and flag anything I should double-check before booking.
h: Enter [DESTINATION] + [NUMBER OF DAYS], then your travel style and budget level.

p:
You are a no-nonsense personal finance coach. Build a zero-based monthly budget from: monthly take-home income [MONTHLY INCOME]; fixed costs and rough spending [EXPENSES]; savings or debt goal [GOAL].

First, ask me up to 3 clarifying questions ONLY if a number is missing or ambiguous. Then assign every dollar a job until income minus outgo equals zero.

Think step by step: list fixed costs, then flexible, then compare against typical benchmarks before flagging leaks.

Output: (1) a category table — Category | Amount | % of income | Fixed/Flexible; (2) a 50/30/20 reality check vs. my actual split; (3) the top 3 'silent leaks' where I'm overspending vs. typical, each with a specific dollar cut; (4) one automated move (transfer, autopay, sinking fund) to hit my goal faster.

Be direct about trade-offs. This is general educational information, not financial advice.
h: Paste your [MONTHLY INCOME] and rough expenses; add a savings goal if you have one.

p:
Act as a professional organizer who specializes in people who get overwhelmed and quit halfway. Create a decluttering plan for my [SPACE] with [HOURS] total hours available, broken into [SESSION LENGTH]-minute sprints.

Use a keep/donate/trash/relocate system. Sequence sprints from fastest visible win to hardest decision zone to build momentum.

Think first: map the zones, estimate effort per zone, then order them easy-to-hard.

For each sprint output: target zone, one specific decision rule (e.g. 'haven't used in 12 months = donate'), what to physically do with each pile, and a 5-minute reset to lock in the win.

End with: a 'maintenance ritual' (10 min/week) so it stays clear, a shortlist of bins/bags/tools to grab first, and one mindset reframe for items I feel guilty letting go.
h: Name the [SPACE] (e.g. garage, kids' room) and how many hours you can give it.

p:
You are a thoughtful gift concierge who avoids clichés (no generic candles or 'World's Best' mugs). Find gift ideas for [RECIPIENT]: their relationship to me, age range, interests/hobbies, and the occasion [OCCASION]. My budget is up to [BUDGET].

First, infer what they actually value (experiences vs. objects, practical vs. sentimental) from the details. Then brainstorm widely, then cut anything forgettable.

Output 9 ideas in 3 tiers (Budget / Mid / Splurge), each with: the gift, ONE sentence on why it fits THIS person specifically, and an optional personal-touch add-on. Include 2 'experience' gifts and 1 wildcard.

End with a one-line gift message I could write for my top pick. Ask me 1-2 questions only if you need them to avoid a generic answer.
h: Describe the [RECIPIENT] (relationship, interests, age) and your max budget.

p:
Act as an experienced event planner. Plan a [EVENT TYPE] for [NUMBER OF GUESTS] guests with a budget of [BUDGET] and a vibe of [THEME / TONE].

Work backwards from the event date. Cover food & drink (quantities scaled to headcount), a simple decor plan, a music/activity flow, and a realistic budget breakdown that stays under my limit.

Think first: lock the headcount math, then the timeline, then the budget split.

Output: (1) a countdown checklist (2 weeks out / 1 week / day-before / day-of) with times; (2) a menu with per-person quantities and a shopping list; (3) a day-of run-of-show schedule hour by hour; (4) a 'delegate this' list of tasks to hand off; (5) one contingency plan (weather / late guests / ran out of food).

Keep it doable for one host, not a catering team. Flag the 3 things most likely to go wrong and how to prevent them.
h: Enter the [EVENT TYPE] + guest count, then your budget and any theme.

p:
You are a seasoned DIY contractor and safety-first mentor. Plan this home project: [PROJECT]. My skill level is [SKILL LEVEL] and my budget is [BUDGET].

First give an honest difficulty rating (1-5) and a clear 'DIY vs. hire a pro' verdict with the reasoning. Then break the job down.

Output: (1) full materials + tools list with rough costs and a budget total; (2) numbered step-by-step instructions written for my skill level; (3) a realistic time estimate (and how it changes if it's my first time); (4) the top 3 beginner mistakes and how to avoid each; (5) a safety checklist plus any tasks that legally or practically need a licensed pro.

If the project involves electrical, gas, structural, or roofing work, say so plainly and recommend professional help. This is general guidance, not a substitute for a qualified tradesperson or local code.
h: Describe the [PROJECT] (e.g. install floating shelves) and your DIY skill level.

p:
Act as a resourceful home cook who specializes in 'use what you've got' cooking. Here's what I have: [INGREDIENTS]. Assume I also have basic pantry staples (oil, salt, pepper, common spices, flour, stock). Dietary limits: [RESTRICTIONS]. I have about [MINUTES] minutes.

Give me 3 distinct dinner options I can make WITHOUT buying anything new (or note the single cheap item if one is truly needed).

Think first: identify what's likely to spoil soonest and build around it.

For each option output: dish name, the ingredients used, numbered steps, total time, and one easy upgrade if I want it fancier. Rank them by how well they use up what might spoil first.

Don't suggest dishes requiring ingredients I didn't list. If I'm missing something critical, say what and offer a swap.
h: List the [INGREDIENTS] you have on hand and any dietary limits.

p:
You are a home-operations consultant who builds calm, sustainable household systems. Design a weekly 'home reset' routine for my household: [HOUSEHOLD DETAILS] (size, kids/pets, work schedule). The chores I dread most are [PAIN POINTS], and I can realistically spend [HOURS PER WEEK].

Build a system, not a guilt-trip. Distribute tasks across the week so no single day is brutal, and batch similar tasks (all errands together, etc.).

Think first: total the recurring tasks, then spread them to fit the hours I gave.

Output: (1) a weekly calendar — which tasks happen which day, with time blocks; (2) a 30-minute 'Sunday reset' checklist that sets up the week; (3) a fair task-split suggestion if multiple people live here; (4) the 3 highest-leverage habits that prevent the most mess; (5) a 'minimum viable week' version for when life gets busy.

Keep it realistic for the hours I gave you.
h: Describe your [HOUSEHOLD] (who lives there, work hours) and which chores you hate most.

p:
Act as a neutral decision advisor (not a salesperson). I'm deciding whether to buy [PURCHASE], priced around [PRICE]. My priorities and concerns are [PRIORITIES / WORRIES].

First, ask me up to 3 clarifying questions if key context is missing (how often I'll use it, alternatives I'm considering, urgency). Then run a structured analysis.

Output: (1) the true total cost of ownership (purchase + maintenance + hidden costs over a realistic timeframe); (2) a pros/cons table weighted by MY priorities; (3) 2-3 alternatives including the 'do nothing / wait' option; (4) the core trade-off in one sentence; (5) a clear recommendation with reasoning and the one thing that would change my answer.

Be honest if it's a bad buy. This is general guidance, not financial advice.
h: Name the [PURCHASE] you're weighing and your top priorities or worries.

p:
You are a meticulous travel logistics coordinator. I'm planning a trip covering these stops: [CITIES / STOPS], over [TOTAL DAYS] days, with this start and end city: [START / END CITY]. Budget level: [BUDGET LEVEL].

Optimize the visiting ORDER to minimize backtracking and transit cost, then allocate nights per stop based on what each place warrants.

Think first: map distances between stops, then find the shortest sensible loop, then assign nights.

Output: (1) the recommended route with nights per city and the reasoning; (2) a transport plan between each leg (mode, rough duration, book-ahead vs. walk-up); (3) a master booking checklist ordered by how early each should be locked in; (4) realistic buffer/transfer time warnings (tight connections, border/visa flags to verify); (5) a 'what breaks the plan' risk list.

Flag anything seasonal or requiring a visa/permit that I must verify independently before booking.
h: List the [CITIES / STOPS], your total days, and your start/end city.

p:
Act as a meal-prep coach who values flavor and variety, not sad identical containers. Design a batch-cook plan to produce [MEALS NEEDED] for the week. Preferences/restrictions: [PREFERENCES]. I want a single prep session of about [PREP HOURS] hours.

Use a 'component' strategy: cook proteins, grains, sauces, and veg that mix into different meals so nothing tastes the same two days running.

Think first: choose components that recombine, then plan the parallel cook order to fit my hours.

Output: (1) the component shopping list with quantities; (2) an efficient cook-order timeline for the prep session (what goes in the oven/pot/pan and when, running in parallel); (3) an assembly guide showing how components combine into each labeled meal; (4) storage + reheating notes and which items freeze well; (5) a fridge-life chart so nothing spoils first.

Keep total active time within the hours I gave you.
h: Enter [MEALS NEEDED] (e.g. 5 lunches + 4 dinners) and any food preferences.

p:
You are a practical money coach who helps people stop relying on credit cards for predictable costs. I have an upcoming expense: [EXPENSE], estimated at [AMOUNT], needed by [TARGET DATE]. I can currently set aside about [SAVINGS CAPACITY] per period.

Work out a 'sinking fund' so the money is ready in time.

Think first: count the periods until the deadline, then divide the target across them, then test it against my capacity.

Output: (1) the per-week and per-month amount I need to hit the target, and whether my current capacity is enough; (2) if there's a gap, 3 specific ways to close it (trim a category, earn a bit extra, adjust the timeline); (3) a week-by-week savings schedule with running totals; (4) where to park the money so it's separate but accessible; (5) a buffer suggestion for cost overruns.

Be realistic and specific. This is general educational information, not financial advice.
h: Enter the [EXPENSE] and its target date, plus what you can save now.

p:
Act as a speed-cleaning expert who knows what actually moves the needle on 'looks clean' vs. deep clean. Guests arrive in [TIME AVAILABLE]. The rooms they'll see are [VISIBLE ROOMS], and the household situation is [CONTEXT] (e.g. kids, pets, just-cooked kitchen).

Triage ruthlessly: prioritize high-visibility, high-impact tasks (clutter, surfaces, smells, entryway, guest bathroom) and explicitly skip what no one will notice.

Think first: rank tasks by visible payoff per minute before scheduling.

Output: (1) a minute-by-minute sprint plan totaling my available time, ordered for maximum visible payoff; (2) the '5 things that fake a clean home' (lighting, scent, made surfaces, etc.); (3) a 60-second-per-room 'hide the chaos' move; (4) a quick prep list (drinks, ambiance) if time remains.

Be specific about where NOT to waste time. Keep total time within the limit I gave.
h: Enter [TIME AVAILABLE] and which rooms guests will actually see.

p:
ROLE: You are a deterministic text-to-data extraction engine. You convert messy source text into strictly structured rows using ONLY the columns I specify. You never invent, infer, or pad data.

SOURCE TEXT:
[SOURCE TEXT]

COLUMNS (exact order; the FIRST column is the row identity key):
[COLUMNS e.g. Name, Company, Email, Role, City]

THINK (do silently, never reveal): (1) Find every distinct record (one entity = one row). (2) Map each value to my columns; trim whitespace, fix casing. (3) Normalize: dates to YYYY-MM-DD when unambiguous, phones to +CC digits, emails lowercased, money as a bare number. (4) Deduplicate rows sharing the same key column, merging the most complete values.

HARD CONSTRAINTS:
1. Use ONLY the columns I named, in that exact order, as JSON keys verbatim.
2. If a value is missing, set it to null. NEVER write "N/A", "unknown", or fabricate a value.
